title: Make right-clicking on unfocused scene tabs possible
body:
Should be merged only after #112904.

This PR allows the right-click menu on a scene's tab to open without the editor also switching to that scene.

Additionally, the following menu options have been adapted to accept the clicked scene as context:
- Save Scene
- Save Scene As
- Show in FileSystem
- Play This Scene
- Set as Main Scene
- Close Tab
- Close Other Tabs
- Close Tabs to the Right

This should help tab management be more frictionless when dealing with complex scenes, especially since the close button only ever appears for the currently focused scene tab.
author: vaner-org

commits.message: Right-click on scene tab without selecting it

title: Improve editor-only class checking
body:
* Closes https://github.com/godotengine/godot-proposals/issues/13986.

Currently, filtering editor-only classes is done by checking for the "Editor" prefix. This leads to false positives for user-defined classes (both scripts and extensions) and false negatives for some built-in exception classes whose names don't start with "Editor" despite being editor-only. But `ClassDB` has an API for more robust detection of editor-only classes.
author: dalexeev

reviews.body: The editor changes seem fine. This also fixes some editor types appearing in CreateDialog (notably GridMapEditorPlugin).

I really like how this is done and think this is go to go except for maybe  `API_EDITOR_EXTENSION` . 

Since we are removing a way for users to hide by prefixing Editor I think this should be followed closely by something along the lines of #91020 or #95806.

Coming back to it I'm wondering if always hiding it is the right solution. Maybe we could have a dropdown to choose what is hidden and what is shown but an addition like that, should come in a latter PR.
Looks great to me!

commits.message: Improve editor-only class checking

title: Fix bottom dock always opening a tab on startup
body:
When a dock doesn't have a tab open, the `dock_#_selected_tab_idx` key is omitted from the config file on save. However, when that key is fetched, a value of `0` is used as a fallback if it's not found, causing the first tab to always be opened.

This PR changes the fallback value to `-1`, and skips the tab opening if the value is below `0`.
author: YeldhamDev

reviews.body:
Tested, fixes the issue. The default layout correctly sets the tabs to 0 too, looks good.
Note loading the default layout closes the bottom dock now, rather than opening the first tab of it. This seems to be the behavior of 4.6.1 as well.

commits.message: Fix bottom dock always opening a tab on startup

title: Reduce `RefCounted` copies in `GDScriptFunction::call`
body:
Related to #117121.

As [talked about](https://chat.godotengine.org/channel/gdscript?msg=pBRsJstLjavoPhqHh) on the contributor's chat, in a GDScript-heavy real-world project that we've been profiling there seems to be a troubling amount of overhead coming from `RefCounted::reference`/`RefCounted::unreference`, a big chunk of which is coming from initializing these `Variant` instances here, as the GDScript VM puts the script and instance references onto the GDScript stack:

https://github.com/godotengine/godot/blob/60fff00a660a2aeb03f88c07fb7baed353bcb72f/modules/gdscript/gdscript_vm.cpp#L656-L663

The reference count increment/decrement that come as a result of this are seemingly entirely redundant, as ~~both~~ the script ~~and instance~~ by definition (?) have to survive the entire function call.

This pull request fixes that by introducing a `VariantInternal::object_assign_raw`, which allows us to (dangerously) write the object pointer/ID/type into the GDScript stack `Variant` without incrementing the reference counter. Then we simply don't run the destructor for these at all, thereby avoiding decrementing the reference counter as well.

I'll see if I can provide some hard numbers from Tracy later on, ~~but we're seeing somewhere on the order of a 10% bump in performance from this in one particular benchmark in this particular project.~~
author: mihe

reviews.body:
Not super well versed with the stuff in the vm, but generally this looks good to me.

I can confirm that I would expect the referencing to be an unnecessary bottleneck, and this is an appropriate fix for it.

Looks good to me, but we should probably add some comments:

1. (`VariantInternal`) That `object_assign_without_ref_unsafe()` should be used with extreme caution.
2. (VM) That destructors for fixed addresses aren't called because `Variant`s are initialized with `object_assign_without_ref_unsafe()`.

Safety rests on several assumptions. One of them is that fixed addresses are never written to, only read. My biggest concern was `NIL`, but after examining the code, I think this assumption is correct (see also #117863). For coroutines everything should be fine too, as far as I understand.
LGTM

commits.message: Reduce `RefCounted` copies in `GDScriptFunction::call`

title: Delegate `Script::reload_from_file` to `ScriptLanguage`
body:
When a `ScriptLanguage` supports documentation, the `EditorFileSystem` will call `Script::reload_from_file` to reload the source code automatically. The problem is that the default behavior of `Script::reload_from_file` assumes the script's source is text-based. While this works for GDScript and C #, it will not work for any language that uses non-text-based storage.

One way to circumvent the issue here is to allow a `ScriptExtension` to override the default behavior of the `reload_from_file` method, enabling the implementation to use language-specific methods to read and set the source code.

The other alternative is to switch `set_source_code` and `get_source_code` to use `Variant`, which I felt was a bit more invasive, but is certainly an alternative to consider if the Godot team would prefer. I would say this change would make far more sense if it also included exposing a way for third-party plugins to register `ScriptEditor`(s), but this is currently something that is only exposed on the engine side.

This PR for now approaches this from the least invasive first option.
author: Naros

reviews.body:
The change makes sense for custom script languages that don't have access to Godot internals. It's also nice to actually have a clear responsibility for those methods defined.

For my purposes the added docs are fine, but if someone from the docs team wants to nitpick, be my guest!

It's worth pointing out that `Script::can_reload_from_file` return `false`, so this method has not many users, the only one I could find is the doc generation so that's were I focused my testing on.

Code looks good to me.

commits.message: Delegate reloading to `ScriptLanguage`

title: Core: Trim `resource_*.h` includers
body:
* Part of #111218 

This PR reduces total includers for `resource_loader.h`, `resource_saver.h`, and `resource_importer.h` (all three are linked and should therefore be handled together for results to be meaningful). 

The 3 commits accomplish the following:
1. Uses @akien-mga 's tool for adding missing includes for all three headers
2. Splits `ResourceFormat*` from `main.h` into `main_resource_format.h` files
  2.1. e.g. `ResourceFormatLoaderCrypto` splits from `crypto.h` into `crypto_resource_format.h`
  2.2. This is purely copy-pasting and then removing the old unnecessary includes from `main.h`. This route was chosen as the `main.h` includers may not necessarily be reducible. This also helps decrease the filesize of all `main.h` that got modified, which will also help a tiny bit with compile time (as we estimate `churn ~= file size x total cpp includers`)
3. Extracts `CacheMode` from `resource_loader.h`'s `ResourceFormatLoader` into `resource_loader_modes.h`
  3.1. `ResourceLoader` and `ResourceFormatLoader` in `resource_loader.h` now redeclare the enum with a combination of `using` for the enum itself and `static constexpr` for its values (we don't have access to `using enum` in C++17).
  3.2. `editor_file_system.h` is updated to use `resource_loader_modes.h` and a forward declared `ResourceImporter`.

Commit 2 is responsible for the bulk of the decrease, although commit 3 is important too due to 3.2, which removes an additional 60~70 includers for all three headers. They are currently split due to doing two very different things, making review hopefully more manageable.

Most of the diff is just from copy-pasting + all the new file copyright headers, with the remaining being include changes. There are no behavioral changes. 
The seemingly unrelated include changes are required for compilation (transitive inclusion my beloved). 

Thanks to these three changes, we get the following include reductions:
* `resource_loader.h` goes from 792 (528cpp) to 241 (190cpp), or -551/-69.5% (-338/-64%)
  * Currently 18th place in fresh compile, and 47th in recompile costs.
* `resource_saver.h` goes from 710 (460cpp) to 141 (97cpp), or -569/-80.1% (-363/-79%)
* `resource_importer.h` goes from 132 (96cpp) to 80 (48cpp), or -52/-39.4% (-48/-50%)

Total includes project-wide (by cpp files only) go from 188654 to 187264, so a 1390, or 0.74%, reduction. 

The remaining transitive includes for `resource_loader.h` and `resource_saver.h` are through `resource_importer.h`, and that one's includers are hard to reduce for very little benefit.

title: Fix exposure not working with canvas background on Compatibility.
body:
Fixes half of #110902.
Alternative to #113863.

Compatibility applies exposure in the scene shader. Since it's not done in post, this means canvas items do not get affected by it since they don't care about the exposure at all.

As a solution, when the exposure is non 1.0 and the background mode is canvas, we can force the internal intermediary buffer to exist and apply exposure to it during the copy operation.

This matches the behavior on Forward+/Mobile.

I tried applying the exposure in the canvas shader instead, but that requires passing down the exposure to the canvas renderer, doing linear/sRGB conversions and also does not work with the background color.
author: blueskythlikesclouds

reviews.body:
This looks excellent! I've tested it and can't find any cases where it doesn't behave as I would hope.

It seems that this intermediate pass that already existed in the code happens just before the glow effect and other post effects, at least for the Canvas background mode? It appears to me that there are some things that happen between this intermediate pass and glow, but those are effects that only apply to non-Canvas background modes, such as Fog.

If you can confirm my understanding is correct @blueskythlikesclouds, then I'd say I have a good understanding of these changes and they all look perfect to me and are ready to merge.

Thanks!
Tested locally, it works as expected. Code looks good to me. Using the MRP from #110902: Renderer | Before | After -|-|- Compatibility | | Forward+ | |

reviews.body: Type switching is still enabled when using custom quick open (`EditorInterface.popup_quick_open(func(what): print(what), ["Texture2D"])`). It shouldn't be.

thanks for adding my proposal, @MartinDelille !

Tested the following (bfcf4562e293609761910336007d095df5df3e63) on intel mbp 15.7:

# Global quick open

## Repeated shortcuts

each correctly simulates a keydown press. 

- global quick open (cmd+p+p+p+p+p release) - works perfectly
- global scene quick open (shift+cmd+o+o+o+o release) - works perfectly
- global script quick open (opt+cmd+o+o+o+o release) - works perfectly!

## Switching types

- Switched between all quick open types using shortcuts, once global quick open was opened - works as expected

# Non-global quick open 

Right click a theme in the inspector-> quick load... 

As expected, no shortcuts caused the dialog's file type filters to change, and no shortcuts caused the 'keydown' keypress simulation to occur. 

## tested all shortcuts:

- global cmd+p: no change of open type, no navigation motion
- global script cmd+opt+o: no change of open type, no navigation motion
- global scene shift+cmd+o: no change of open type, no navigation motion

really nice improvement :)
Works correctly now.

title: Improve drag and drop in `Tree`
body: Closes #54400, closes [#3201](https://github.com/godotengine/godot-proposals/issues/3201). This PR improves intuitiveness and usability for moving nodes via drag and drop in `Tree`, by: 1. Adding an always-present vertical component to the drop indicator, that points up to a to-be parent in the future place of their relationship line, as well as a phantom dropdown arrow when about to be the first ever child of a node. 2. Considering the x position of the cursor in determining to-be parent when in indent space (negative space to the left), akin to functionality seen in vector design software. When in item space, the current behaviour that users expect is largely maintained. https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/a7ba81d9-3d81-4e9d-b646-d94a0dd895d4 3. Moving the drop indicator drawing outside of `draw_item`, and placing it on its own CanvasItem, that StyleBoxes that could previously occlude it no longer do. | Before | After | | ------------- | ------------- | ||| 4. Preventing invalid drop targets, so unhandled cases of dropping as a descendant of self are no longer possible. These changes allow for much more fluid interactions with the tree, where any kind of rearrangement the user might want to do is possible within a single mouse click, while also more clearly telegraphing relationships post-release. cc @kleonc
author: vaner-org

title: Revamp autoload creation

- Text fields are replaced with buttons:

  - Create Scene Autoload - same as above, but creates a scene. The UX is a bit awkward, because you need to go through 2 dialogs, but maybe it's fine

title: EditorQuickOpenDialog: Rewrite internal paths usage and clean history
body:
Fixes #114835

**The Issue:**

The EditorQuickOpenDialog internally uses resource paths instead of uids and keeps a history of previously selected items. When a file is moved or removed the old path is injected into the search results through this history.

**The Fix:**
This PR rewrites the internal path usage to uids fixing issues with files renaming, and adds a check in the _get_history method to ensure ids are still valid, fixing issues with files removed at runtime.
author: LucaTuerk

reviews.body: Still getting some errors You should filter out invalid UIDs in `_find_uids_in_folder()`. I have some junk resources in my test project that come with no UID, which is what triggers the errors.

There are some code problems, but overall works fine now.
The main problem is that you added another cleanup in `_get_history()`, which should be unnecessary.

title: Fix clipboard history not updating on subsequent copies in Wayland
body:
<!--
Please target the `master` branch in priority.

Relevant fixes are cherry-picked for stable branches as needed by maintainers.

To speed up the contribution process and avoid CI errors, please set up pre-commit hooks locally:
https://contributing.godotengine.org/en/latest/engine/guidelines/code_style.html
-->

Fixes #116522 

When setting the clipboard, the data source was reused after the first copy and only the in-memory buffer was updated, so the compositor was never notified of subsequent clipboard changes.

The change makes it destroy and recreate the `wl_data_source` when setting the clipboard selection, so that `wl_data_device_set_selection` is called each time. Clipboard managers (e.g. KDE Klipper) detect new entries by receiving a new selection event from the compositor, which only fires when set_selection is called with a new data source.

This PR also applies the same fix to the primary clipboard.
author: gongpha

reviews.body:
Fixes the issue, but someone needs to review the Wayland code itself.

Really hope this get merged though, such an annoying issue.

Code looks good!

Could not see any obvious regressions on my usual test harness (KDE 6.5.4, GNOME 49.2.2, COSMIC 1.0.1), and my laptop (sway 1.11).

commits.message: Fix clipboard history not updating on subsequent copies in Wayland

title: Add scroll switch to ScrollContainer
body:
Sometimes with UI related stuff, you want the scroll wheel to control the horizontal scroll by default. Right now the approach is to switch it manually through GDScript but with this PR you can "flip the switch" and have the mouse wheel scroll horizontally instead of vertically without having to hold shift. It basically swaps the axes.

A perfect use case for me personally would be for the timeline for the video editor I'm making with Godot, but I'm certain that there are other instances where being able to flip the scroll axes with a switch could be useful. So I thought this might be a good feature to add to the ScrollContainer node.

I wasn't certain about the naming of the option. Let me know if anything needs adjusting/changing. Btw, learned from my previous PR and added documentation with the first commit this time ;)

title: `RWLock`: use C++17's `shared_mutex` instead of timed variant
body:
C++17 (the current standard used by Godot, soon to be upgraded to C++20) added `shared_mutex` as a cheaper version of C++14's `shared_timed_mutex` that leaves timed features out.

`RWLock` uses none of `shared_timed_mutex`'s timed features (any functions using `_for` and `_until`), so it's completely safe for the mutex to be a `shared_mutex`.

(Considering Godot was already using C++17 at the time, I wonder why `shared_mutex` wasn't used in #45314. If it was some compatibility issue at the time, recent tests don't show anymore.)
author: DeeJayLSP

reviews.body: I don't remember why I neglected `shared_mutex`. Maybe I wasn't aware we had upgraded to C++17. Maybe, because of something else. Whatever the case, this change makes total sense.

commits.message: `RWLock`: use C++17's `shared_mutex` instead of timed variant

title: Only print to stdio before setup and during/after cleanup
body:
Fixes #117789.

This introduces a new `CoreGlobals::print_ready` global, which when disabled will force anything that goes through `_err_print_error`, `__print_line`, `__print_line_rich` or `print_error` to only print to the stdio streams (`stdout`/`stderr`).

This lets us enable this flag when entering `Main::setup`, and disable it when entering `Main::cleanup`, allowing us to avoid all the problematic `ERR_*` calls during shutdown that would otherwise try to interact with `Object`/`Variant` machinery and whatever else that could have already been torn down at that point.

title: [Accessibility] Fix `update_set_name` setting a space character as the node label when name and `name_extra_info` is empty
body:
When both name and name_extra_info are empty, the resulting name is a single space character. Screen readers may then speak out the word 'Space' because it is one character (occurs with ORCA v46).

## Reproduction
Set everything on empty on a control node. Then using espeak-ng and ORCA (tested with v46), focus on the node. The read-out will be "Space".

```
DisplayServer.accessibility_update_set_name(rid, '')
DisplayServer.accessibility_update_set_description(rid, '')
DisplayServer.accessibility_update_set_role(rid, DisplayServer.ROLE_UNKNOWN)
DisplayServer.accessibility_update_set_tooltip(rid, '')
DisplayServer.accessibility_update_set_value(rid, '')
DisplayServer.accessibility_update_set_role_description(rid, '')
```

ORCA will be silent when you set the name as a space ' '. Then ORCA recognizes 2 or more characters and treats it differently. Here is that branching code for ORCA: https://gitlab.gnome.org/GNOME/orca/-/blob/main/src/orca/speechdispatcherfactory.py#L545

## AI Use
Claude helped me identify the problem and point me to the relevant code files in Godot, Accesskit, and ORCA. Claude's fix was naive and left trailing spaces. strip_edges() should take care of that.

## Background
I need elements to be ignored by the screen reader because I have voice actors speaking the text. For nodes that are accounted for with a voice from a different source, a screen reader must remain silent. Currently the only way to do this is to set everything to an empty string and set role to ROLE_UNKNOWN. ROLE_CONTAINER would be better but this will not work with some screen readers (ORCA), it will read out as 'Section'. But that is a different bug.

P.s. this is my first contribution and I'm figuring out how to do this. I just read that making a PR through github is generally not accepted unless it is a small fix. I deemed this pretty small and confined. But if desired I'll fork and fix it locally.

<!--
Please target the `master` branch. We will take care of backporting relevant fixes to older versions.

Before submitting, please read our checklist for new contributors:
https://contributing.godotengine.org/en/latest/engine/introduction.html#checklist-for-new-contributors
-->
author: Josvanegmond

commits.message:
Fix (accessibility): strip spaces and only set label if full name is not empty.

When no name and extra info is set, it would still add a space. is_empty() would never be true. The space character would be set as label and screen readers are prompted to read it with various output. Fixed by stripping spaces.

title: [macOS/Windows] Remove dynamically linked ANGLE support, add flag to enable/disable ANGLE.
body:
Supersede https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/109889
Same as https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/117313 for ANGLE.

- Adds `angle` build flag to fully enable/disable ANGLE (defaults to `true`.
- If libs are missing, warning is printed and `angle` is set to false.

author: bruvzg

reviews.body:
Tested locally on Linux + WINE, it works as expected.

I've seen very little use of dynamically linked ANGLE in Godot projects in the wild, so I agree with this change.

Note that this is a "soft dependency", and compilation will continue with ANGLE disabled if the libraries haven't been downloaded. This is similar to AccessKit, but different from Direct3D 12 which will abort compilation unless you pass `d3d12=no` explicitly. I think this makes sense considered Compatibility isn't used as often as Forward+/Mobile on desktop, but maybe there is an argument to making it a hard dependency like D3D12 (with `angle=no` needing to be explicitly passed if missing).

title: Use IDs instead of hashes for TrackCache keys
body:
Fixes: https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues/116231

Hello, 

I've been tracking this issue since `4.6.0-stable` made it apparent. This is a solution that implements re-hashing on hash collisions for `track_cache`. As far as I'm aware, using `StringName` for hash was the latest solution proposed here (https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/110439) by @beicause for hash collisions. I pulled the last commit there and tested it, and found it decreasing the performance a bit. `uint32_t` comparisons are faster so I wanted to keep them.

In order to both fix the collision issue and keep using `uint32_t`, I made 3 implementations and tested them:
1. Dynamic double hash (Using double `AHashMap`)
	- `track_cache` holds `(TypeHash, HashBucket *)` pairs where `HashBucket` contains a `union` that is either a `TrackCache *` or another `AHashMap`
2. Adding a linked list in `TrackCache` struct
	- the `struct` simply holds `next` and `prev` pointers
	- adds to the list in case of collisions and iterates the whole list per lookup
3. Re-hashing the hash that collides with a `uint32_t probe` value until it no longer collides within the `track_cache`
	- Nothing changes, just the `thash` value is probed when a collision is detected
	- All hashes are inserted into a static `AHashMap` and that map is checked during insertion

These solutions are either proposed or mentioned in other hash related PR discussions: ([the one referenced above](https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/110439) and https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/110400)(https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/110439).

I tried not to touch other parts of the code and only focus on hash. My benchmark results for the provided MRP in (https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/110474) with my system (Ryzen5 1600, RTX2060) is as follows:

  | Stable | Dynamic Double Hash | Rehash | Linked List | StringName
-- | -- | -- | -- | -- | --
Avg FPS | 127.72 | 116.38 | 127.5 | 124.84 | 110.04
Avg MSPF | 7.83 | 8.59 | 7.84 | 8.01 | 9.08

Since that mrp does not contain any hash collisions, these can be considered as amortized performance. 

All except rehashing, requires additional checks to see if the hash key maps to a unique track or a bucket; and another lookup after that in case of collisions. So as expected, their runtime performances are worse (more than I expected). Also, collisions occur very rarely, so making additional checks that costs a few frames for a very rare case made no sense to me. Therefore, moving the cost to track creation time seemed like the best option. 

My implementation is not exactly what @TokageItLab proposed (with deferred calls and all) because I didn't understand how to do that :laughing:.

I tested this PR with [mrp-rehash.zip](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/25722014/mrp-rehash.zip) . It contains 3 tracks in animation player that generates the same hash in v4.6. Adding/removing, undo/redo, works fine in my tests. I tested these only in 2D.
author: ugursoy

commits.message:
Use StringName pointer as IDs for TrackCache keys, instead of hashes
- Store StringName in Track

title: Add support for joypad motion sensors
body:
Closes https://github.com/godotengine/godot-proposals/issues/2829
Partially supersedes https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/88590
Requires https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/111707 to be merged first

I decided to split my big SDL3 joypad features PR ( https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/107967 ) into several smaller PRs, starting with joypad motion sensors.

This PR adds the ability to get a joypad's motion sensors (accelerometer, gyroscope) data readings (and also a simulated gravity sensor from the accelerometer and gyroscope data). The reason the gravity sensor had to be simulated: https://github.com/libsdl-org/SDL/issues/6555#issuecomment-1320918918
This PR also includes the methods to calibrate the motion sensors output. For calibration and gravity calculation, this PR uses https://github.com/JibbSmart/GamepadMotionHelpers

This PR was tested on Windows, but it should also work on Linux and macOS.

TODO:
- [x] Mention that it's recommended to disable the motion sensors if they're no longer needed because it can theoretically drain the controller's battery more quickly
- `InputEventJoypadGyroscope` and `InputEventJoypadAccelerometer` (not sure)
- [x] `Input.get_joy_sensor_rate()`?
- [x] Use GamepadMotionHelpers library for calibration
- [x] Include the accelerometer gravity part in both calibrated and non-calibrated modes (sorry for being indecisive 😅)
- [x] Invert the sensors axes (see also https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/113635 )
- [x] Mark the `has_joy_*`, `set_joy_*_enabled` and `is_joy_*_enabled` as experimental (we may or may not need to only enable/disable the motion sensors together in the future, I think)
- [x] Modify the usage example to use 3D nodes
- Accelerometer usage example (not sure it's necessary)

TODO for separate PRs:
- [ ] Accelerometer/gyroscope events
- ~~Accelerometer events for Input Map (Joypad Accelerometer Up/Down/Left/Right)~~ I got feedback for this idea from Calinou, he suggested not to include those.
- [x] Motion sensors tutorial for the documentation (accelerometer should also be included) ( https://github.com/godotengine/godot-docs/pull/11692 )

Test project: (EDIT: it currently uses outdated method names)
[joy_motion_sensors_test.zip](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/24411470/joy_motion_sensors_test.zip)
You should lay the controller on your desk before running the project, and after "Calibration is done" message is printed you can pick up the controller.
author: Nintorch

reviews.body:
Tested locally, I can't get gyro or accelerometer to be enabled on a DualSense Edge (wired) on Linux. I've tried both bundled SDL and system SDL. It works on a standard DualSense (wired) though.

Wired Switch Pro Controller also works, but it continuously drifts upwards when I do nothing (I've tried unplugging and replugging the controller several times). This didn't occur on https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/107967#pullrequestreview-3012257702.

I'll test this on Windows/macOS later today.

**Edit:** DualSense and DualSense Edge (wired) both work on Windows 11 24H2. I can't get the Switch Pro Controller to be recognized at all though (not specific to this PR). Note that if you unplug the controller during gameplay, the last gyro motion will be retained, which results in the view continuously drifting off until you replug the controller and re-enable gyro.

macOS also works with DualSense and DualSense Edge (wired), but the Switch Pro Controller isn't recognized at all either.

**Testing project:** [physics_interpolation_gyro.zip](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/23911248/physics_interpolation_gyro.zip) (modified from https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/107967#pullrequestreview-3012257702 to only test features available in this PR)

Also, the gyro direction appears to be inverted compared to https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/107967#pullrequestreview-3012257702. In the project I linked, both X and Y aiming are inverted with all controllers. Is that intended?

Code and documentation look good to me.

The DualSense Edge's sensors not working on Linux isn't a huge issue in my book (since it's a rare controller on among PC users in general, and even more so on Linux). Therefore, I don't consider it to be a blocker for this PR, especially since it may be an upstream SDL issue.

Happy to! I'm looking forward to getting to do cool controller stuff with Godot projects 😄 

(And I realized I hadn't technically approved this through GitHub yet, which isn't strictly necessary since I'm not a maintainer... but I suppose it's good to put my stamp of support behind it anyways, so here!)
Tested with DualSense controller, seem to be working fine. Code looks good.

commits.message: Add support for joypad motion sensors

title: Fix GLES3 batching skipping rendering all items on specific buffer sizes
body:
Fixes #117602.

As far as I've tested, also:
Fixes #105645.
Fixes #107297.

When adding last instance to the batch, it instantly starts another batch and resets the index to zero. So when the batched instance count was a multiple of the `max_instances_per_buffer` then because of the `if (index == 0)` check it would skip rendering all the batched instances.
AFAICT a correct check is to ensure we're still at the first batch and that its instance count is zero.
author: kleonc

commits.message: Fix GLES3 batching skipping rendering all items

title: Fix tangent decoding detection when computing vertex skinning
body:
Fixes #81446

When decoding veretx tangent in the shader, there is a special case to detect which encoding system to use (Octahedral or axis-angle coordinates). See scene.glsl, line 618.
In the issue mentioned above, some octahedral encoded tangents are exactly (0, 1) which is the value used to detect the encoding. When this special value is detected, we switch to axis-angle decoding, instead of Octahedral, resulting is undefined results. This only happened for skinned objects.

The fix is the same as the c++ code (rendering_server.cpp line ~650) : detect this special case when encoding the tangent in the skeleton compute shader, and apply a different but equivalent encoding instead.

I did not modify the gles3 skeleton shader.  I could be wrong, but the compatibility mode does not seem to suffer from this.

For the fix, we could also check the uint value directly for slightly fewer instructions, but I feel this would make the code harder to maintain in the future.
author: Gaktan

commits.message:
Fix Octahedral encoding special case when computing vertex skinning

Signed-off-by: Gaktan <Gaktan@users.noreply.github.com>

title: Add unique Node IDs to support base and instantiated scene refactorings
body:
The main goal of this PR is to safeguard when a base or instantiated scene changes (nodes renamed, moved or readded), that the hierarchy is still maintained and the node can be found.

Supersedes #86960.

What it does:
* Implements unique node IDs.
* These IDs act as a fallback to names when saving.
* The IDs are **USED AS A FALLBACK**, so they are just an addition. It should not break any current existing scene.
* If a scene renames or moves a node, inherited or instantiated scenes will no longer lose reference to it.
* This should also eventually allow for 3D asset importing that supports unique IDs to keep references in case of scene inheritance used.

Unlike the previous approach, this one is intended to be a fallback, only used if the node is not found. This makes it safer to implement and ensure that, at worst case, we fail to find the node, but nothing breaks.

*Bugsquad edit:*
- Fixes #19520
- Fixes #41047
- Fixes #82700
- Fixes #84928
- Fixes #97576
- Fixes #100038
- Fixes #110518
- And fixes a handful of closes bug reports and proposals for the same issue
- Closes https://github.com/godotengine/godot-proposals/issues/6291
author: reduz

reviews.body:
General question: Is it really safe to fall back to unique IDs when the parent instantiated scene changes? What if the structural changes imply a change to how the nodes should be stored? 
Perhaps _trying_ to restore is better than not trying, but it doesn't feel foolproof.

Looks good now.

A nice thing is that scenes with ids can still be opened in older versions of Godot (which also means that this can be safely reverted if problems arise, but I didn't spot anything after some testing).
Looks great to me. This solves one of the biggest pain points users have had with inherited and instantiated scenes over the past 10 years!

commits.message:
Add unique Node IDs to support base and instantiated scene refactorings

The main goal of this PR is to safeguard when a base or instantiated scene changes (nodes renamed, moved or readded),
that the hierarchy is still maintained and the node and its overridden properties can be preserved.

What it does:
* Implements unique node IDs.
* These IDs act as a fallback to names when saving.
* The IDs are **USED AS A FALLBACK**, so they are just an addition. It should not break any current existing scene.
* If a scene renames or moves a node, inherited or instantiated scenes will no longer lose reference to it.

Unlike the previous approach, this one is intended to be a fallback, only used if the node is not found.
This makes it safer to implement and ensure that, at worst case, we fail to find the node, but nothing breaks.

title: Wayland: Improve mapping robustness and synchronization
body:
Fixes #115739.
Should fix #104752. (Didn't replicate on any of my usual compositors)

We assumed that a window will be ready after a single roundtrip but that's actually not guaranteed by the XDG shell protocol.

This patch waits for an arbitrary timeout (10s) before erroring out and forcefully closing the window. Since this exercised quite some code paths, it also reworks some window handling logic and fixes a bunch of synchronization issues.

---

Tested with Pinnacle 0.2.2 (built from commit `b6c40da68024fb7fbb1db1f0890a87b08da013c9`) on my KISS Linux laptop by following the steps from the [linked issue](https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues/115739). Couldn't notice any obvious regression on my KISS Linux laptop with sway 1.11 and my Fedora 43 VM with KDE 6.5.4, GNOME 49.2.2, and COSMIC 1.0.1.

Note that this solution isn't ideal as the world stops while waiting. We should obviously make everything asynchronous but I'm not entirely sure how much that is possible.

If the timed out window is the main window the engine will fall back to X11 properly, although for some reason *only in this case* `DetectPrimeX11` (if invoked) stalls. I'd really postpone this issue to another PR if possible, given how specific it is. (Does it even count as a regression? :P)
author: deralmas

commits.message:
Wayland: Improve mapping robustness and synchronization

We assumed that a window will be ready after a single roundtrip but
that's actually not guaranteed by the XDG shell protocol.

This patch waits for an arbitrary timeout (10s) before erroring out and
forcefully closing the window. Since this exercised quite some code
paths, it also reworks some window handling logic and fixes a bunch of
synchronization issues.

title: Use `TRACY_ON_DEMAND` by default for Tracy integration
body:
Fixes #115798. As per Tracy's manual ([PDF here](https://github.com/wolfpld/tracy/releases/latest/download/tracy.pdf)) Tracy will accumulate events even before you've connected a client to it. This accumulation can be hundreds of megabytes per second for non-trivial applications, pretty much forcing you to have Tracy running (and have it set to use a memory limit) if you don't want to cause crashes or severe slowdowns of your system due to running out of memory. However, Tracy offers a `TRACY_ON_DEMAND` compile definition that stops it from accumulating these events when there isn't a client connected:
[Click to expand/collapse]

Therefore, this pull request adds a new build option called `profiler_record_on_demand`, which maps to `TRACY_ON_DEMAND`, allowing you to omit `TRACY_ON_DEMAND` by disabling this new option. This new option is enabled by default, meaning `TRACY_ON_DEMAND` is now enabled/provided, since the current behavior without it seems very costly, especially since even when the Tracy client is connected its memory allocations seem quite intense, effectively limiting how long you can record samples for in general, with or without this new build option:
[Click to expand/collapse]
author: mihe

reviews.body: Makes sense to me.
Makes sense to me, I trust you checked that it improves the situation overall.

commits.message: Use `TRACY_ON_DEMAND` by default for Tracy integration

title: Fix plugin signal registration error on Android
body:
I see these two errors in the log:

```
ERROR: Condition "!Thread::is_main_thread()" is true.
   at: add_signal (core/object/gdtype.cpp:116)
```

```
ERROR: In Object of type 'JNISingleton': Attempt to connect nonexistent signal 'launch_tests' to callable 'Node2D(main.gd)::_launch_tests'.
    at: connect (core/object/object.cpp:1535)
    GDScript backtrace (most recent call first):
       [0] _ready (res://main.gd:9)
```

Android plugins signals are registered at

https://github.com/godotengine/godot/blob/4a280218fcfdd69408cceb74577c9e69086be23a/platform/android/plugin/godot_plugin_jni.cpp#L94-L113

https://github.com/godotengine/godot/blob/4a280218fcfdd69408cceb74577c9e69086be23a/platform/android/api/jni_singleton.cpp#L70-L77
author: syntaxerror247

reviews.body:
This is not how `GDType` is intended to work.
Adding signals should only happen in `_register_methods`, which should only happen during the app launch lifecycle on the main thread. 
Doing it elsewhere would be a violation of typing semantics / coherence, and would lead to problems when we further try to optimize and consolidate the type system.

Could you explain why the plugin system is trying to register signals from another thread, outside `_bind_methods`?
_(for readers' context: All other systems in Godot are required to register everything in `_register_methods` as well, as are GDExtensions)_

I can't speak to the lifecycle of the java parts, but I can confirm this solution is no longer blocking for me.
Let me know if we can do anything to improve the situation / interplay further.

commits.message: Fix plugin signal registration error on Android

title: GDScript: Fix compiler generates incorrect bytecode for conversion return
body:
This PR fixes a bug I noticed during review #115763:

```gdscript
func test(object: Object) -> Node:
    return object

func output(value: Variant) -> void:
    if value is Object:
        var object: Object = value
        print("<%s>" % object.get_class())
    else:
        print(var_to_str(value).replace("\n", ""))

func _run() -> void:
    # Before: Prints `<Resource>`.
    # After: Runtime error `Trying to return a value of type "Resource" from
    #                       a function whose return type is "Node".`.
    output(test(Resource.new()))
```

The reason was that type compatibility checking was performed in the code generator and had a bug (it generated `OPCODE_RETURN` instead of `OPCODE_RETURN_TYPED_NATIVE`).

I removed the duplicate bytecode generation code (the first branch was missing checks for the `GDScriptDataType::{NATIVE,SCRIPT,GDSCRIPT}` cases) and moved the conversion check logic to the analyzer; the compiler passes the result to the code generator using the `use_conversion` field (similar to `use_conversion_assign` in `AssignableNode`, `AssignmentNode`, and `ForNode`). The code generator isn't actually designed for type checking because it deals with `GDScriptDataType` instead of `GDScriptParser::DataType`.

Test script:

```gdscript
class A:
    func f1() -> Node: return null
    func f2(x: Object) -> Node: return x
    func f3(x: Node2D) -> Node: return x
    #func f4(x: Resource) -> Node: return x # Analyzer error.
```

Bytecode (before):

```
Function f1()
 0: line 2:     func f1() -> Node: return null
 2: return const(<null>)
 4: == END ==

Function f2(x)
 0: line 3:     func f2(x: Object) -> Node: return x
 2: return stack(3)
 4: == END ==

Function f3(x)
 0: line 4:     func f3(x: Node2D) -> Node: return x
 2: return stack(3)
 4: == END ==
```

Bytecode (after):

```
Function f1()
 0: line 2:     func f1() -> Node: return null
 2: return const(<null>)
 4: == END ==

Function f2(x)
 0: line 3:     func f2(x: Object) -> Node: return x
 2: return typed native (const(class(Node))) stack(3)
 5: == END ==

Function f3(x)
 0: line 4:     func f3(x: Node2D) -> Node: return x
 2: return stack(3)
 4: == END ==
```

---

I also made the VM error messages more consistent and complete (using the `_get_var_type()` function). Given that I didn't have to update other unit tests, it appears there were no tests at all for returning values ​​prior to #115763.

---

Finally, this PR slightly optimizes the bytecode for return statements that return a typed array or dictionary of the same type as the function's return type. In this case, we can simply use `OPCODE_RETURN` instead of `OPCODE_RETURN_TYPED_ARRAY` or `OPCODE_RETURN_TYPED_DICTIONARY`, since type checking is not required at runtime.

Test script:

```gdscript
func f(x: Array[int]) -> Array[int]:
    return x
```

Bytecode (before):

```
Function f(x)
 0: line 2:     func f(x: Array[int]) -> Array[int]: return x
 2: return typed array stack(3)
 7: == END ==
```

Bytecode (after):

```
Function f(x)
 0: line 2:     func f(x: Array[int]) -> Array[int]: return x
 2: return stack(3)
 4: == END ==
```
author: dalexeev

reviews.body:
This does indeed fix the mentioned issue.

Code looks good to me.

commits.message: GDScript: Fix compiler generates incorrect bytecode for conversion return

title: Fix WindowWrapper leaking all key inputs to parent
body:
- Fixes https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues/104758
- Fixes https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues/108594
- Fixes partially https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues/106285 - the script editor no longer takes the events, but the game doesn't either

This happened with any floating window that didn't accept the input and any node that used key input or non-global shortcuts in the parent window.
The cause was this hack in WindowWrapper:

https://github.com/godotengine/godot/blob/56f3e2611daadb0b1894b46737ee3000e82e33f9/editor/gui/window_wrapper.cpp#L66-L67

WindowWrapper's ShortcutBin was pushing inputs to parent windows for global shortcuts, but this was being treated as normal input for TextEdit and all other nodes. Only shortcuts should be sent to the parent window, and we can't just filter to InputEventShortcut, since InputEventKey is also used for shortcuts.
I added a parameter to Viewport to handle this and logic just for shortcuts. It is not exposed for now, but may be useful generally.
We may also want to use this for popup menus in the future, so global shortcuts can still work when they are open.

I ignore the parent viewport if it doesn't use `handle_input_locally`, since it isn't needed in the Editor.
author: kitbdev

reviews.body: Fixes the issues on my end. But needs a rebase.

commits.message: Fix WindowWrapper leaking all key inputs to parent

title: Improve inspector clipboard
body: Somewhat a follow-up to #111469 This PR changes inspector clipboard from a single Variant to a struct that also keeps clipboard type. E.g. when you copy a section, the clipboard type is set to SECTION, so you can't paste it to categories or properties. Previously this information was stored inside the value itself. Also makes the Paste option disabled if the clipboard type does not match: Notably, the inspector clipboard type is initially EMPTY, so you can't use paste until you actually copy something (previously it would paste null).

title: Automatically remove incomplete file downloads
body:
When HTTPRequest fails to download a file (either by download error or manual cancel), the file is saved anyway and is incomplete (corrupted). There is no way to resume the download AFAIK, so there is no reason to keep the file.

This PR makes incomplete files automatically deleted. I don't know much about HTTPRequest, so not sure if there isn't another code path for successful downloads, but I tested that it works correctly with simple requests.

This change is needed for #117072. I tried manually removing the file after failed/canceled request, but it does not work for whatever reason 🤷‍♂️
author: KoBeWi

commits.message: Automatically remove incomplete file downloads

This new bottom editor comes with the following features: - Presentation of items in a grid, with search and zooming. - Editing of individual items in a separate inspector. - Full undo/redo for all actions. - Fallback preview to an item's mesh in case none was specifically set. Supersedes #116736. Supersedes #115281. --- Some extra changes were necessary for this PR: - A new `MeshLibrary` method: `get_item_count()`. - Exposure of `Resource.copy_from()` (as `copy_from_resource()`, to not clash with `Image.copy_from()`).

title: Fix undoing terrain set move duplicates terrain in second set
body:
Fixes: #116636 

## Bug Preview

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/4b831ab7-a54d-4aeb-95d6-5b948c5990c2

## Cause

The issue was caused by the undo system restoring terrain properties without clearing the existing terrain container first. Because of this, the previous terrain state was effectively applied on top of the current one, leading to duplicated or mismatched terrain data.

## Fix

This PR fixes the problem by clearing the terrains in the affected terrain sets before restoring their properties during undo. This ensures the terrain containers are rebuilt from the saved undo state instead of being merged with the current state.

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/b3c86048-a57b-47e3-be3d-8d36ebdea7c4
author: filipemrabreu

commits.message:
Fix #116636: Undoing terrain set move duplicates terrain in second set

Undoing a terrain set move could corrupt terrain data where a
terrain from the first set appears duplicated in the second set.

This happens because UndoRedo restores terrain properties without
clearing the existing terrain container first, causing the previous
state to be overlaid on top of the current one.

Fix this by clearing the terrains in the affected terrain
sets before restoring their properties during undo.
This ensures the terrain container is rebuilt from the
saved properties instead of merging with the current state.

title: Stop autocomplete from eating words by default
body:
Fixes https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues/101306

basically undoing the changes from https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/90723
author: Goldenlion5648

reviews.body: Approving on principle. I believe the feedback we've gotten since the initial change has made clear, that the current defaults cause more friction.
I've felt the annoyance this fixes.

Makes sense to me too.

In general I feel actions with an extra modifier (here Shift) should be the more complex/least obvious actions, and I think this PR corrects this.

> basically undoing the changes from https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/90723

Note that this does not undo https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/90723 , previously the behavior was that tab and enter did different actions, that PR made them the same, and this PR doesn't change that. This PR just swaps the shortcut that shift is needed for.

Like I mentioned here https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues/101306#issuecomment-2797420403 , #90723 changed the behavior to match VSCode but VSCode has different behavior for different languages. For GDScript, the default is actually insert.

I agree that insert is usually more expected than replace too.
Tested, works as expected.

title: Dynamically check for 3D editor snapping state
body:
This change simplifies the check for snapping when we're editing the transform of a `Node3D`. It should address godotengine/godot#117497.

*Bugsquad edit:* Fixes #117497.

As of writing, we only update `Node3DEditorViewport::EditData::snap` in `Node3DEditorViewport::begin_transform`. This works well when the user toggles snapping before modifying a `Node3D`'s transform.

That said, if the user is pressing and releasing the Ctrl/Cmd key while adjusting a transform, snapping won't necessarily enable/disable.

~~To address this, we update the state of `snap` in `Node3DEditorViewport::update_transform`. This allows the user to toggle snapping while adjusting a `Node3D`'s transform.~~

We now check the snapping state dynamically. See [below](https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/117606#issuecomment-4094744134).

## Notes
No generative AI was used in the research, development, or testing of this PR's changes.
author: danbolt

reviews.body:
Works, code looks fine.

Axis locking still works, `Use Snap` still works (even if you change the shortcut).

**Note:** This also changes the behavior of `Ctrl` when `Use Snap` is enabled to **_NOT_** use snap, but this makes sense and aligns with how Blender handles things as well.

title: Fix timeline cursor following mouse during marker selection
body:
When right-clicking an existing marker and then left-clicking an empty space on the timeline, and click another place on the timeline, the timeline cursor follow mouse without holding mouse.

Reproducible in: 4.6.1.stable
Not reproducible in: 4.5.1.stable

Before fix:

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/2fe84dc3-50f4-420c-93a4-a0d5289d6aec

After fix:

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/baaa50f1-8ebf-442e-a86b-8de184f25f29

Similar to #117276, The bug is reproducible in 4.6.1.stable while not reproducible in 4.5.1.stable. The bug was introduced in commit 2a10291. I confirmed that 2a10291~1 doesn't have this problem. The cause of the bug is that `moving_selection_attempt` is set as true in `_try_select_at_ui_pos`, and `AnimationMarkerEdit` accept the mouse releasing event when `moving_selection_attempt` is true, so the timeline doesn't know left-mouse has been released. To solve this problem we can add a `moving_selection_attempt = false`.

MRP
[mrp-timecursor-follow-jump.zip](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/26114886/mrp-timecursor-follow-jump.zip)

title: Fix hint string parsing for nested dictionaries
body: Fixes #104259 Helps #107556 Changes the inspector's hint string parsing to only split on the first `;` instead of all instances which caused the nested hint strings to get decimated and not passed down correctly. This allows typed dictionaries to be nested as the value via property list manipulation. For easier to define nested collection support, see #107556 . This would also be fixed with the hint string parsing used in #112632, but I went with the minimal change while the other PR is in review. **Note**: This does not help defining a nested dictionary as a key, only a value. Supporting that kind of hint string would need some kind of grouping syntax to know which `;` belongs to which level of the nesting. This PR is only trying to improve the more common use case. title: Improve options for orienting particles in space
body:
This PR:
- adds local billboarding (X or Y axis)
- fixes billboarding ignoring custom axis
- adds rotation velocity3d

Supersedes https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/116363 and https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/113838

While working on the axis rotation, I had some doubts about the implementation. In particular, we are given feedback over and over regarding how confusing the placement of our properties is. I felt like rotation around axis being placed with the billboards was going to be very counter intuitive for people. Initially, I just wanted to verify that adding 3d rotation velocity on top of axis rotation would still work with the velocity alignmnet done in the copy shader. And, well, it didn't.

Between the unintuitive property placing and the fact that adding 3d rotation velocity later would not have been compatible with the work I did, i decided to remove angle over life in transform align for 3d and just add 3d rotation velocity. I'm not happy that I couldn't give something better than what other particle systems offer, but if anything this explains why it's done that way. 

It's gone a long distance from the initial proposal ( https://github.com/godotengine/godot-proposals/issues/13747 ) but this is what needs to be implemented for the feature to be the easiest to use it can.

title: OpenXR: Allow using Vulkan subsampled images with foveated rendering
body:
When using foveated rendering on Vulkan, we are using a fragment density map (FDM) from OpenXR that tells the GPU to render some parts of the frame in a lower resolution (generally the parts around the edge, or outside of the area the player's eye is looking at when eye-tracking is available).

However, as far as I understand it, in Godot's current implementation all of the output pixels in the low resolution areas still get set (perhaps by copying the pixels multiple times to fill in the lower resolution areas?).

But if we switch to rendering to Vulkan subsampled images, it'll store the data in a such a way that it doesn't store all the output pixels, and is somehow better optimized for tile memory. Or, something - I'm not a rendering expert :-)

**This has been shown to produce a nice performance boost on both Meta Quest 3 and Samsung Galaxy XR in [my fork of the GDQuest TPS demo](https://github.com/dsnopek/gdquest-tps-demo/tree/standalone-vr-benchmark).**

## Benchmarks

My benchmarks fix the camera at 3 specific spots in the scene and gather some stats. 

As you can see below, there is a reproducible ~1ms improvement in frame times!

### Meta Quest 3

#### Before

| Marker  | Frame Time (ms) | FPS |
| ------- | --------------- | --- |
| Marker1 | 14.64           | 68  |
| Marker2 | 17.36           | 57  |
| Marker3 | 13.89           | 72  |

#### After

| Marker  | Frame Time (ms) | FPS |
| ------- | --------------- | --- |
| Marker1 | 13.89           | 72  |
| Marker2 | 15.79           | 64  |
| Marker3 | 13.89           | 72  |

### Samsung Galaxy XR

I ran the Meta Quest tests at 72fps, but the FDM provided by OpenXR on Samsung Galaxy XR is much more extreme than on Quest, so I had to run them at 90fps in order to show the difference.

#### Before

| Marker  | Frame Time (ms) | FPS |
| ------- | --------------- | --- |
| Marker1 | 12.28           | 82  |
| Marker2 | 13.66           | 73  |
| Marker3 | 11.13           | 90  |

#### After

| Marker  | Frame Time (ms) | FPS |
| ------- | --------------- | --- |
| Marker1 | 11.17           | 90  |
| Marker2 | 12.54           | 80  |
| Marker3 | 11.11           | 90  |

## Issues

### ~~Meta Quest~~

~~Unfortunately, this PR doesn't work on Meta Quest: instead of rendering correctly, the full frame is a blurry gradient of color that changes subtly depending on the direction you are looking.~~

~~There is a variant of this PR that _does_ work on Meta Quest, and that's where only Godot's render buffers use subsampled images but the OpenXR swapchain doesn't (see the `@todo` comments in the PR). This runs fine!~~

~~However, in that variant, I don't see any performance improvement on either Meta Quest or Samsung Galaxy XR, so it wouldn't be worth doing.~~

**UPDATE:** Meta Quest is working now!

### ~~Sampling the color or depth texture~~

~~Everything works fine if we never sample the color or depth texture, either in a custom shader, or by using an effect or feature that will cause Godot to switch from using a subpass for tonemapping to using a normal render pass (which will happen if you enabled FXAA or glow, for example).~~

~~I think this could be fixed by using a sampler that's created with the `VK_SAMPLER_CREATE_SUBSAMPLED_BIT_EXT` flag, but I haven't figured out a good way to do that in Godot's renderer yet.~~

~~One option, is we just say we don't support subsampled images in that case (which is what's currently documented in this PR). However, it would be great to allow all features to work.~~

**UPDATE:** Now, subsampled images will be automatically disabled if any incompatible rendering features are enabled.
author: dsnopek

reviews.body: The code looks good to me!
Had some text left in the buffer.. oops :)

commits.message: OpenXR: Allow using Vulkan subsampled images with foveated rendered

title: Renderer: Simplify shader and cluster builder
body: Simplify cluster_builder code - Apple devices leverage MSAA for improved cluster builder quality - `gl_HelperInvocation` + MSAA is the root cause of the rendering artifacts. - Disable the `gl_HelperInvocation` check using a specialisation constant, which removes two variants from compilation - Remove `NO_IMAGE_ATOMICS` variant, as the macros are not used in cluster_render.glsl, removing a further two variants. > [!IMPORTANT] > > I didn't observe any noticeable impact on perf. For reference, the rendering artefacts looked like the following when MSAA is enabled and `gl_HelperInvocation` is used:
author: stuartcarnie

commits.message:
Renderer: Simplify shader and cluster builder

- gl_HelperInvocation + MSAA is the root cause of the rendering
  artifacts.
- Disable the gl_HelperInvocation check using a specialisation constant,
  which removes two variants from compilation
- Remove NO_IMAGE_ATOMICS variant, as the macros are not used in
  cluster_render.glsl, removing a further two variants.

title: EditorFileDialog: Document native file dialogs in sandboxed environments
body:
<!--
Please target the `master` branch. We will take care of backporting relevant fixes to older versions.

Before submitting, please read our checklist for new contributors:
https://contributing.godotengine.org/en/latest/engine/introduction.html#checklist-for-new-contributors
-->
In the `EditorFileDialog` documentation, it states that `FileDialog.use_native_dialog` is ignored and editor settings `interface/editor/appearance/use_native_file_dialogs` will be used instead to determine if native file dialog should be used [[1]](https://github.com/godotengine/godot/blob/4a280218fcfdd69408cceb74577c9e69086be23a/doc/classes/EditorFileDialog.xml#L8). However `EditorFileDialog::_should_use_native_popup` will return true when Godot detects it is running in sandbox, ignoring said editor settings[[2]](https://github.com/godotengine/godot/blob/4a280218fcfdd69408cceb74577c9e69086be23a/editor/gui/editor_file_dialog.cpp#L74). Such behaviour is not documented in `EditorFileDialog` nor `EditorSettings`, and might confuse macOS and Linux users running Godot editor in a sandbox.

This PR simply copy the note about sandbox from `FileDialog.use_native_dialog` to `EditorFileDialog` description, which should correctly reflects the current behaviour of `EditorFileDialog`.

Related issue: https://github.com/flathub/org.godotengine.Godot/issues/187
author: j20001970

reviews.body: This is correct. On macOS, native dialog is the only way for sandboxed app to gain access to the file system outside the sandbox (in case of Linux, it depends on specific sandbox and app configuration).

commits.message: EditorFileDialog: Document native file dialogs in sandboxed environments

title: SCons: Update SCU to accelerate new folders
body:
Adds some of the newer folders to the SCU build.

## Discussion
While #106272 attempted to deal with some of the conditional compilation cases (e.g. `disable_physics_2d`), this PR is simpler and less controversial, and just continues with the existing paradigm and adds some of the newer folders.

This leads to more modest gains, but still worth having.

In some ways with the gradual use of more folders in master, it would be nice to move to a more recursive folder approach, but currently we still mostly manually add `SCsub` files for each folder and this stays with that paradigm, which means periodically adding / removing folders from `scu_builders` to keep the builds working as fast as they can.

## Build Times
_(on my PC, absolute times vary according to hardware, but relative times should be ballpark indicative)_
| Build | Master (Regular) | Master (SCU) | PR (SCU) |
| ----- | ----- | ---- | ---- |
| Full Rebuild | 5:54 | * | 3:16 |
| Without ThirdParty | 4:29 | 2:08 | 1:52 |

## Notes
* There's some platform specific stuff I haven't added yet as I'm currently linux only (e.g. DirectX12) but these could probably be added too in the long run. I can't really test non-linux stuff easily because the CI only runs SCU for one of the builds afaik.
* Also moves around a few of the existing folders in `scu_builders` to make it more alphabetical so easier to follow.

title: Add `View3DController` for editor 3D view manipulation
body:
Currently, both the `Node3DEditorPlugin` and `RuntimeNodeSelect` classes share duplicate code in order to handle multiple things, such as camera manipulation, node selection, etc. This PR is the first step into decoupling that code into unified helpers that both can use, beginning with 3D view manipulation.

`View3DController`, as the name says, allows for the manipulation of the 3D view. With the help of "Cursors", which are used to store transform data to be given to `Camera3D`s. This class allows operations such as panning, orbiting, looking, and others.

This PR already adds some missing features to the in-game editor, such as:
- Editor-set shortcuts for view manipulation.
- Use of the navigation schemes.
- Camera snapping when orbiting.
- Inertia.

Keep in mind that `Node3DEditorPlugin` features are deeply coupled, which makes it a slow and complex process to separate them into something that can be used standalone. So it's for the best to add those modifications in small chunks.

I would also ask for reviewers that make use of the 3D editor to test this exhaustively before merging. 3D camera editing/debugging is an important part of the engine, and the last thing I want to do is to cause regressions to it.

Bug squad edit: Fixes: https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues/117721
author: YeldhamDev

reviews.body:
I'm not that familiar with 3D workflow, but I did test this with the 3D platformer demo and the camera seems to be working correctly.

The code looks fine, it's mostly moving things away from the editor, like ViewPanner.
Haven't tested but code looks good!

commits.message: Add `View3DController` for editor 3D view manipulation

title: Fix glow visual compatibility regression
body:
Reverts the default value of Environment.glow_hdr_threshold from 0.0 back to 1.0 to restore the expected glow appearance in existing projects.

The default was inadvertently changed from 1.0 to 0.0 in PR #110077, which caused glow effects to render dramatically different across all rendering methods (Forward+, Mobile, and GL Compatibility). This broke backward compatibility with existing projects like the Kenney 3D Platformer starter kit.

Setting the value back to 1.0 aligns with documented recommendations and restores visual consistency.

Fixes #112469

<!--
Please target the `master` branch in priority.

Relevant fixes are cherry-picked for stable branches as needed by maintainers.

To speed up the contribution process and avoid CI errors, please set up pre-commit hooks locally:
https://contributing.godotengine.org/en/latest/engine/guidelines/code_style.html
-->

reviews.body:
You need to change the default value everywhere, not just in Environment.h

Is one place:
https://github.com/godotengine/godot/blob/cb3af5afff15669a684ba4f04b9aa5c70dfd09ad/servers/rendering/storage/environment_storage.h#L106

You should look at the original PR to find the other places where it needs to be changed. 

After that, you need to run doctool to update the documentation. How to do that is described here: https://contributing.godotengine.org/en/latest/documentation/class_reference.html#updating-class-reference-when-working-on-the-engine
Looks fine to me. Thanks for making the requested changes!

commits.message:
Fix glow visual compatibility regression (issue #112469)

Reverts the default value of Environment.glow_hdr_threshold from 0.0
back to 1.0 to restore the expected glow appearance in existing projects.

The default was inadvertently changed from 1.0 to 0.0 in PR #110077,
which caused glow effects to render dramatically different across all
rendering methods (Forward+, Mobile, and GL Compatibility). This broke
backward compatibility with existing projects like the Kenney 3D
Platformer starter kit.

Changed files:
- scene/resources/environment.h
- servers/rendering/storage/environment_storage.h
- drivers/gles3/effects/glow.h
- drivers/gles3/rasterizer_scene_gles3.cpp
- doc/classes/Environment.xml

Setting the value back to 1.0 aligns with documented recommendations
and restores visual consistency.

Fixes #112469

title: Remove raw base pointer from `GDScript`
body:
Currently GDScript stores its base once as `Ref<GDScript>` (`base`) and once as raw `GDScript*` (`_base`). They are always set/unset together, the raw pointer is just assigned `base->ptr()`. So the value of those should always be the same.

The comment on `_base` reads as if the raw pointer was cached for performance. However `Ref::ptr` is at the moment force inlined and just returns the raw pointer that the ref uses. So based on my knowledge it should be equally as fast. Both members are present since 0b806ee so maybe ref did have some overhead at some point.

Main advantage is preventing invalid states and to remove noise from the code.
author: HolonProduction

reviews.body:
Looks good to me.

Note that `_FORCE_INLINE_` does not generally guarantee inlining: for dev and size-optimized builds, the `inline` keyword is used instead of an attribute, so the compiler will determine whether to inline based on its own heuristics. However, the compiler will likely always inline `ptr()`, since it is a trivial accessor.

https://github.com/godotengine/godot/blob/cb3af5afff15669a684ba4f04b9aa5c70dfd09ad/core/typedefs.h#L76-L84

https://github.com/godotengine/godot/blob/cb3af5afff15669a684ba4f04b9aa5c70dfd09ad/core/object/ref_counted.h#L124-L126
Makes sense!

commits.message: Remove raw base pointer from `GDScript`

title: Wayland: Fix use-after-free of `Ref<ColorManagementProfile>`
body:
Fixes #117676

PR #111964 made a Ref<ColorManagementProfile> which we intentionally leaked across a client-server barrier owned, which caused a use-after-free. Manually adding reference/unreference calls keeps the ptr valid without memory leaks.

title: [MacOS] Fix non-deterministic framework linking order on macOS for incremental builds
body: ## Issue On macOS, the order of `-framework` flags in LINKFLAGS can vary between builds due to the use of unordered data structures for `extra_frameworks`. This causes the flags string generated from the SCons environment to change on every build invocation, even when there are no actual configuration changes. For module developers using external build systems (like Rake) that consume these flags, this results in: - Unnecessary regeneration of external build configuration files - Loss of incremental build capabilities for the external system ## Solution Sort the `extra_frameworks` list before generating the framework linker flags to ensure a deterministic order. ```python # Before frameworks = [item for key in extra_frameworks for item in ["-framework", key]] # After frameworks = [item for key in sorted(extra_frameworks) for item in ["-framework", key]]
author: LanzaSchneider

reviews.body: Seems reasonable to me!

commits.message: fix: Fix non-deterministic framework linking order on macOS for incremental builds

title: Fix BlendTree appearing empty
body:
An early return in `update_graph_immediately` can lock `graph_update_queued` as `true` forever, rendering `update_graph_immediately` impossible for `update_graph` to call again, due to its own early return when `graph_update_queued` is true, causing all BlendTrees to appear empty until the engine is restarted.

This bug is very difficult to reproduce, but I'm positive I've encountered it twice since the optimisation PR.
Hopefully a glance at the file makes this make sense.
author: vaner-org

commits.message: Fix early return preventing BlendTree graph update

title: Make `memnew(RefCounted)` return `Ref`, to improve ownership safety
body:
This PR improves `RefCounted` ownership semantics for newly created objects, preventing potential crashes and other footguns at build time.

## Background

When `memnew` initializes `RefCounted` subclasses, they start with an (effective) allocation count of 0.

This creates two ownership related problems:

- When stored as `RefCounted *` initially, some code may increase and later decrease the refcount (e.g. when passing as `Variant`). This destructs the object, because the refcount is decreased to 0. The actual owner's `RefCounted *` will be a dangling pointer. The owner should have stored `Ref<RefCounted>` instead.
- When in the `postinitialize_handler` / `NOTIFICATION_POSTINITIALIZE`, `RefCounted` objects still have an allocation count of 0, because the caller of `memnew` didn't claim a reference yet. If the allocation count is increased and later decreased during this function, the object will unexpectedly destruct (see https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues/108395).

The ideal (only?) way to fix this is to start `RefCounted` with a refcount of 1. The `memnew` caller must take ownership of the refcount after the call. This just means we return `Ref` from `memnew`. Most callers already store the result in a `Ref`, so they are compatible with this change.

## Overview

Implementing the change was fairly straight-forward; I add and specialize `memnew_result_t` such that for `RefCounted` types, `memnew` returns `Ref<T>`.

The rest of the changes are fixing current ownership problems. Mostly, it just involves changing `T *` to `Ref<T>`. It has some inherent risk, but I don't think it's huge.
author: Ivorforce

reviews.body:
This needs a rebase to address conflicts in the tests.

However, I'm strongly in support of this change! The core functional changes look good to me. And I've reviewed all the conversions where we were keeping refcounted objects as a pointer, and they all look good to me

commits.message: Make `memnew(RefCounted)` return `Ref`, to force callers to take ownership of it through a reference.

title: Revert "[.NET] Remove EFS update on reloading assemblies" but with deferred call
body:
- Fixes https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues/116169

It turns out this call was needed after all, it ensures new scripts are included in the global class list by updating EFS for them. In my previous PR (https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/108487) I must've only tested scripts that have been opened or loaded by the editor at some point so I didn't encounter the bug.

This reverts the https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/108487 but with a deferred call to avoid reintroducing the bug that the PR fixed (https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues/104052). Updating EFS here is still too early, so we defer the call to ensure the type info is available.

This reverts the previous PR but with a deferred call to avoid reintroducing

title: Add fast path to Polygon2D
body:
This saves a huge amount of time when animating vertices of a Polygon2D manually

Fixes: https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues/115476
Fixes: https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues/74540

The fundamental problem is that previously we recreate the internal mesh every frame. With this change we simply update the mesh each frame instead. This new approach is still not optimal, but it gets us most of the potential performance while being very simple and safe. What makes it not optimal is that we are recalculating all the surface data each time, and updating the entire buffer even if only a portion of the buffer needs to be updated. 

An optimal approach would be to go back to the Godot 3.x way of doing things and introduce a new rendering command to render polygons dynamically. The problem with this is it adds a lot of complexity to Polygon2D and would require a new rendering codepath in the renderer. In practice, it probably isn't worth the added complexity when we get most of the benefit with this simple change

So far I have tested this on an M2 MBP. 

MRP from https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues/115476: 33 FPS -> 101 FPS

MRP from https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues/74540: 80 objects -> 220 objects
author: clayjohn

reviews.body:
Tested locally, it works as expected. Code looks good to me.

## Benchmark

<details open>

<summary>PC specifications</summary>

- **CPU:** AMD Ryzen 9 9950X3D
- **GPU:** NVIDIA GeForce RTX 5090
- **RAM:** 64 GB (2×32 GB DDR5-6000 CL30)
- **SSD:** Solidigm P44 Pro 2 TB
- **OS:** Linux (Fedora 43)
</details>

Using a release optimized export template build (`production=yes lto=full`).

MRP from https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues/115476: 75 FPS -> 179 FPS

MRP from https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues/74540: 172 objects -> 351 objects

commits.message:
Add fast path to Polygon2D

This saves a huge amount of time when animating vertices of a Polygon2D manually

dynamic global removal logic.

title: Fix incorrect reading of joypad UTF8 `raw_name` in `Input.get_joy_info()`
body:
I didn't know that `SDL_GetJoystickName()` can return a UTF8 string, but I just tried one of my DirectInput controllers and in Godot 4.5 it returns a partially garbled `raw_name`:
```gdscript
	print(Input.get_joy_name(0), " ", Input.get_joy_guid(0))
	print(Input.get_joy_info(0))
```
```
NEXT SNES Controller 0300c1871008000001e5000000000000
{ "raw_name": "(Ð¡Ñ^BÐ°Ð½Ð´Ð°Ñ^@Ñ^BÐ½Ñ^KÐµ Ñ^AÐ¸Ñ^AÑ^BÐµÐ¼Ð½Ñ^KÐµ Ñ^CÑ^AÑ^BÑ^@Ð¾Ð¹Ñ^AÑ^BÐ²Ð°) usb gamepad", "vendor_id": "2064", "product_id": "58625", "xinput_index": "0" }
```
With this fix `raw_name` is now readable (in this case, to a Russian speaker):
```
NEXT SNES Controller 0300c1871008000001e5000000000000
{ "raw_name": "(Стандартные системные устройства) usb gamepad", "vendor_id": "2064", "product_id": "58625", "xinput_index": "0" }
```
(For anyone curious, the text in parentheses reads "Standard system devices")
author: Nintorch

commits.message: Fix UTF8 joypad raw_name in get_joy_info()

title: Fix symbol jump history navigation issue.
body:
- Fix #92420

- Related #97031
- Related #108057

This PR aims to make the history navigation behave like it does in VS Code/Visual Studio.

There are several main issues in the original code:
1. Each time a script is opened (jump) from other script, its old state will be saved to history, which is unnecessary.
2. A calling of `_save_history()` is unnecessary, as saving is auto triggered by caret changed signal.
3. `TabContainer` and other `Container`s are calling deferred to re-fitting their children. The original code used some `call_deferred` to try as much as possible to `adjust_viewport_to_caret`/`center_viewport_to_caret`. However, sometimes only one tick delay is not enough to get the editor ready for these operations.


before:

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/39badd73-c9b5-4601-b354-f7c1fc136dfe


https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/b62a067f-7e91-44e0-a3f4-b59a8377e515


after:

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/193745d2-2c16-42b0-b696-5cf0bb7adef0


https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/8f74f9ae-ad8a-459a-8a20-c5719d6be0b1


MRP:
[test_static_set_2026-02-21_22-50-19.zip](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/25459011/test_static_set_2026-02-21_22-50-19.zip)
author: jinyangcruise

reviews.body: I can confirm that it fixes the issue, but I'm not sure about all the changes to Control class, and especially exposing them. If they are absolutely necessary, they can be internal. Although sounds like it's just a workaround for script editor overusing deferred calls (or maybe it's the "proper" fix that's supposed to avoid the deferred calls?).

The resulting behavior is quite obviously desired from a usability perspective, but I think the code review is what matters most here as there may be unexpected changes in behavior that I haven't found while trying this PR. I'm not knowledgeable enough about the underlying code.
Seems ok overall. The newly added methods are fully internal, so they are fine.

commits.message: fix symbol jump history navigation issue.
