title: Profiler: Fix for graph using excessive CPU
body:
There's a bug in the profiler graph code that is causing excessive CPU usage.
This bug effects the _update_plot() method (and other places) which is what draws the graph.
This will be very helpful with #117066 

*** This fix results in a 4X improvement in draw speed for the profiler graph! ***

Here are some numbers for the _update_plot() code:
Before the fix
_update_plot 100 runs total:   551381 usec, average:   5513 usec per call - graph is starting to fill up
_update_plot 100 runs total: 1323278 usec, average: 13232 usec per call
_update_plot 100 runs total: 1809684 usec, average: 18096 usec per call - graph is full from here on
_update_plot 100 runs total: 1869551 usec, average: 18695 usec per call
_update_plot 100 runs total: 1873438 usec, average: 18734 usec per call
_update_plot 100 runs total: 2002019 usec, average: 20020 usec per call
_update_plot 100 runs total: 2016659 usec, average: 20166 usec per call

After the fix
_update_plot 100 runs total: 165619 usec, average: 1656 usec per call - graph is starting to fill up
_update_plot 100 runs total: 343914 usec, average: 3439 usec per call
_update_plot 100 runs total: 527919 usec, average: 5279 usec per call - graph is full from here on
_update_plot 100 runs total: 577738 usec, average: 5777 usec per call
_update_plot 100 runs total: 584657 usec, average: 5846 usec per call
_update_plot 100 runs total: 578274 usec, average: 5782 usec per call
_update_plot 100 runs total: 503249 usec, average: 5032 usec per call

1 line code change in 2 files. So simple.

Technical information. Changed from passing by value to passing by reference. When you pass by value the system implicitly makes a temporary copy of the data structure. This particular data structure is fairly complicated so a deep copy is expensive from a CPU perspective. Additionally the system must allocate memory for each temporary copy. Also this code is called many, many times in the graph drawing code so it really adds up from a CPU and memory usage viewpoint. 

AI special thanks. I would never have found this issue without the AI. I asked it to look for optimization possibilities in the drawing code specifically and it pointed out the pass by reference vs value issue. Using that information I put in timing code to get before times, fixed the 2 lines, and ran it again to get the after fix times. The previous developers had tried to capture this using the & for their variables but without the change to the method signature those attempts were not effective. Because everyone else missed it this demonstrates how AI can be an amazingly effective check on your code.

*******************************
Disclosure and License
-As a software engineer I use many systems to ensure code quality, and that includes AI. I use the AI to: do detailed code analysis, be a sounding board for my different ideas, look for bugs, find unexpected behaviors, identify performance hot spots, expensive CPU calls, race conditions, poorly placed variable initializations, possible optimizations, and many more things. I do this during development and before I push code to the repo. It lists items that are potential problems and I decide what I think is important and make those changes.
-For a descriptive example of my AI usage on a particular PR please read this https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/117066#issuecomment-4061773098
-Any and all code attached to this PR falls under the coverage of the MIT License.
author: uoslider-wq

reviews.body:
Oof, that's a heavy copy.
Looking at caller code it was clearly intended to return a reference, so this should be fine.

commits.message: Profiler: Fix for graph using excessive CPU

title: [TextEdit] Fix clipping of last character due to right margin rounding
body:
This fixes an off-by-one clipping issue introduced in commit 1794098.
Using ceil() on the right margin reduces the visible text area by up to 1 px,
causing the last character of long lines to be clipped.

This restores the previous behavior by using floor() for SIDE_RIGHT while
keeping ceil() for SIDE_LEFT.

- Fixes #110577.

See also:
-  #114221
- https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/110791

This pull request resolves the issue without affecting other behavior: it only adjusts the one extra pixel needed at the end of `TextEdit` on custom scales so the last character is visible again. Probably using floats is a proper solution (#114221), but if that is not planned in the near future, this is a simple, low-risk improvement to the currently severely impacted usability.
author: Anyone4551

reviews.body:
Fixes the issue, does not reintroduce #105827.

We should still merge #114221 at some point, it fixes an issue with the minimap and makes the behavior a bit more consistent with other controls.
This issue gets reported a lot, so going with this for now the fastest way to get it fixed. This should also be cherry-pickable for 4.6.

commits.message: [TextEdit] Fix right margin rounding clipping last character

title: Move signal ownership from `ClassDB` to `GDType`
body:
- Follow-up of #113586 
- Needs and includes #111262 
- Works towards https://github.com/godotengine/godot-proposals/issues/12323

Moves signal ownership from `ClassDB` to `GDType`, to further consolidate type inspection into `GDType`.
After enum info, signal ownership is the next easiest thing, which is why I'm starting with it to further establish the pattern.

~Draft until #111262 is merged.~

## Explanation

After #113586, this is (luckily!) a fairly uninteresting PR:
- Moves `signal_map` from `ClassDB::ClassInfo` to `GDType` as `self_signal_map`.
- Adds `signal_map` to `GDType` as a cache of all signals throughout the hierarchy (for performance purposes). This can optimize signal lookup immensely.
- Rejects signal registration attempts after the type is finalized.

LGTM

Code changes look good to me.
Tests pass and doctool isn't broken so I assume the intended functionality is still working :)

title: Restrict condition parsing in shader preprocessor
body:
This PR intended to solve a huge part of https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues/96504.
I've overloaded an `Expression` by a new `ShaderExpression` class to avoid code duplication.

Restricted from usage:

- Built-in functions

I suppose there is still a room for improvement, but I think this is a good start to limit this chaos.

title: Improve source-generated `EmitSignal{...}` performance in C#
body:
There are two `EmitSignal()` overloads:
- `public Error EmitSignal(StringName signal, params Variant[] @args)`
- `public Error EmitSignal(StringName signal, ReadOnlySpan<Variant> @args)` (added in #96329 by DE-YU)

Calling the first one (using `params`) is not ideal since it allocates an array (unless no arguments passed). Calling the second one allows a stack-allocated span. Using `[]` allows the second one to be called, which is prioritised by C#.
author: Joy-less

reviews.body: I agree that micro-benchmarks don't always translate to real-world performance gains. However, I think the change here is small enough to accept it. In general, it sounds like a good idea to prefer the span overloads in C# where it makes sense, and I think this change fits the criteria.

commits.message: Improve source-generated `EmitSignal{...}` performance

title: Fix unknown identifier `source_texture` in `texture_blit` visual shader
body:
Fixes #117019

This PR corrects the naming of `source_texture` inputs for `Shader::MODE_TEXTURE_BLIT` in visual shaders. 
Previously, the inputs had an inconsistent naming scheme (e.g., `source_texture`, `source_texture2`). 
This resulted in `unknown identifier "source_texture"` when sampling from one of the source textures.
The naming of the uniform declarations has been copied (`source_texture0`, `source_texture1`, `source_texture2`, `source_texture3`), resolving those errors.

[Apple Embedded] Fix static .a/.xcframework library loading in open_dynamic_library

When a .a or .xcframework path is requested but not found on disk (because
it is already statically linked into the binary), the previous code set
`path = String()` and fell through to `dlopen("")`, which is undefined
behavior and bypasses the RTLD_SELF symbol lookup table used by
`get_dynamic_library_symbol_handle`. This caused GDExtension loading to
fail silently or with ERR_CANT_OPEN when building with vulkan=no on iOS.

Fix by returning RTLD_SELF early, consistent with the existing empty-path
case at the top of the function.

`T read()` method of the `RingBuffer` checks the `space_left()` instead of `data_left()`, causing it to read empty buffers and not reading full ones.

title: Fix macOS Steam time tracking lost when opening a project
body:
Fixes #112281.

On macOS, when the Project Manager opens a project, it spawns the Editor via `NSWorkspace`. The `NSWorkspaceOpenConfiguration` object doesn't carry over the parent's environment by default — it gets a clean one from `launchd`. This causes `SteamAppId` (set by the Steam client) to be missing in the Editor process, so `SteamAPI_Init()` silently fails and Steam shows "no longer running."

This passes the parent process environment through to the spawned app instance, matching the behavior on Windows (`CreateProcessW` inherits by default) and Linux (`fork`/`execvp` inherits by default). Both the modern `NSWorkspaceOpenConfiguration` path and the legacy `launchApplicationAtURL:` path (x86_64 pre-10.15) are fixed.
author: Arthurmtro

reviews.body: Makes sense, the other platforms always use parent process environment.

commits.message: [macOS] Pass parent environment to spawned app instances

title: Add UTF-8 encoding to svg file open in `platform_builders.py`
body:
This PR fixes the build failure on Windows by explicitly specifying UTF-8 encoding when reading SVG files in platform/platform_builders.py, preventing the UnicodeDecodeError caused by the system default GBK encoding.

When building Godot on Windows, the build process fails with a UnicodeDecodeError during the generation of SVG icon headers for platform exporters. This happens because platform/platform_builders.py reads SVG files without explicitly specifying UTF-8 encoding, causing Python to fall back to the system default encoding — which is GBK on Chinese Windows installations.

Windows systems configured for Chinese locales use GBK as the system default encoding, while SVG files are universally stored in UTF-8 and often contain multi-byte special characters that cannot be decoded correctly by GBK. This encoding mismatch results in an immediate decoding failure and halts the build.

The specific error message is as follows:
```
UnicodeDecodeError : 'gbk' codec can't decode byte 0xaa in position 174: illegal multibyte sequence 
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "C:\Users\...\SCons\Action.py", line 1434, in execute 
    result = self.execfunction(target=target, source=rsources, env=env) 
  File "...\platform\platform_builders.py", line 14, in export_icon_builder 
    svg = file.read() 
UnicodeDecodeError: 'gbk' codec can't decode byte 0xaa in position 174: illegal multibyte sequence
```
author: DSOE1024

commits.message:
Add UTF-8 encoding to svg file open in platform_builders.py

Specify UTF-8 encoding when opening the source file.

title: iOS: Propagate VC UI preferences to SwiftUI hosting controller
body:
Fixes #116632
Fixes https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues/115552

The SwiftUI lifecycle migration (#109974) wraps `GDTViewController` inside a `UIViewControllerRepresentable`. The hosting controller becomes the window's root view controller, but it doesn't delegate `preferredScreenEdgesDeferringSystemGestures`, `prefersHomeIndicatorAutoHidden`, or `prefersStatusBarHidden` to child VCs. Since iOS queries the root VC for these, the corresponding Godot project settings (`suppress_ui_gesture`, `hide_home_indicator`, `hide_status_bar`) are silently ignored.

This adds `propagateUIPreferencesToRootViewController` which copies `GDTViewController`'s existing method implementations onto the hosting controller's class using `class_addMethod` / `method_setImplementation`, called from `viewDidAppear:` when the window hierarchy is established.

Guarded with `#ifdef IOS_ENABLED` since visionOS doesn't use these APIs.

<!--
Please target the `master` branch in priority.

Relevant fixes are cherry-picked for stable branches as needed by maintainers.

To speed up the contribution process and avoid CI errors, please set up pre-commit hooks locally:
https://contributing.godotengine.org/en/latest/engine/guidelines/code_style.html
-->
author: jaypaik

reviews.body: Methods seems to be called as expected, I do not have any old device to test if it's fixing the issue.

commits.message:
iOS: Propagate VC UI preferences to SwiftUI hosting controller

The SwiftUI lifecycle wraps GDTViewController in a hosting controller
that becomes the window's root VC. iOS queries the root VC for
preferredScreenEdgesDeferringSystemGestures, prefersHomeIndicatorAutoHidden,
and prefersStatusBarHidden, but the hosting controller doesn't delegate
these to child VCs. This propagates the preference implementations onto
the root VC's class at runtime using class_addMethod.

title: [Apple Embedded] Fix static .a/.xcframework library loading in `open_dynamic_library`
body:
## Problem
When building iOS with vulkan=no, GDExtension loading fails with ERR_CANT_OPEN.

The static library fallback in open_dynamic_library sets path = String() and calls dlopen(""), which doesn't search the statically linked symbol table. The fix returns RTLD_SELF early instead, consistent with the existing empty-path case already handled in the function.

Tested on iOS with vulkan=no.
author: ssd3

commits.message:
[Apple Embedded] Fix static .a/.xcframework library loading in open_dynamic_library

When a .a or .xcframework path is requested but not found on disk (because
it is already statically linked into the binary), the previous code set
`path = String()` and fell through to `dlopen("")`, which is undefined
behavior and bypasses the RTLD_SELF symbol lookup table used by
`get_dynamic_library_symbol_handle`. This caused GDExtension loading to
fail silently or with ERR_CANT_OPEN when building with vulkan=no on iOS.

Fix by returning RTLD_SELF early, consistent with the existing empty-path
case at the top of the function.

title: Set accessibility name on Tree inline cell editor when editing
body:
## Summary

Fixes #110417.

When editing a `Tree` item inline — e.g. renaming a node in the Scene dock via **F2** — a `LineEdit` (or `TextEdit` for multiline cells) appears inside a `Popup`. This editor had no `accessibility_name` set, so screen readers had nothing to announce when it received focus.

For comparison, the search box in the **Add Node** dialog works correctly because it has a `placeholder_text` which `LineEdit` uses as its accessible name (see `NOTIFICATION_ACCESSIBILITY_UPDATE` in `line_edit.cpp`).

**Fix:** in `Tree::edit_selected()`, set the accessibility name on `line_editor` / `text_editor` before `grab_focus()`:
- Use the column's translated title (`xl_title`) if one is set.
- Fall back to the item's current text (e.g. the node name) so the screen reader announces context like *"Node2D, edit text"*.

Both the single-line (`CELL_MODE_STRING`) and multiline cases are fixed.

## Notes

- The column title fallback to item text is intentional: the Scene dock tree has no column title, so using the node name gives the user clear context about what they are editing.
- Tested that the fix compiles cleanly. Runtime verification with a screen reader (NVDA/Narrator) is needed to confirm the announcement behavior.
author: marcomolinaleija

reviews.body: Please squash the commits, see [The interactive rebase](https://contributing.godotengine.org/en/latest/organization/pull_requests/creating_pull_requests.html#the-interactive-rebase).

title: CI: Add `#include` validation hook
body:
- Related #117491

In the above PR, it was noted that there's no `clang-format`/`clang-tidy` option to forbid relative includes. I remembered one of my earlier PRs playing around with an `#include` validator to ensure encapsulation, and was able to re-implement that idea as stylistic enforcement instead. In doing so, this new hook revealed nearly 300 instances of non-module files using relative includes!

As a stylistic validator, I also threw in two other checks that felt relevant: forbidding Windows-style paths, and ensuring thirdparty includes use angle-brackets. No manual adjustments were made after the validation steps, to give a clearer idea on what *just* the validator (and subsequent `clang-format` run) results in

title: `AudioStreamWAV`: Check for `eof_reached` when reading LIST INFO tags
body:
Fixes #116099. Supersedes #116112 which touches code beyond the issue.

By analyzing the file, it seems to have a `LIST` chunk that not only is placed after `data` (which is usually the last), but also informs a size of 3,225,026,560 bytes, which is far beyond the end of the file.

This is an unusual case, but it can be solved by simply adding an `eof_reached` check that breaks the `INFO` loop (which honestly, should have been there long ago).

It will print an error in the editor, but it's reasonable for a file with invalid chunks.

title: Style: Define clang-format rules for include grouping
body:
So clang-format now supports defining advanced rules for include grouping, and it seems to work fairly well.
I've tried to codify our [header include guidelines](https://contributing.godotengine.org/en/latest/engine/guidelines/cpp_usage_guidelines.html#header-includes) using it, with a few adjustments that may warrant discussion and adjusting the docs (but now that clang-format takes care of it we don't need to document with too much detail).

I'll add some review comments to point out discussion topics.
author: akien-mga

reviews.body:
I think these changes make sense, the grouping is much clearer this way

Automating some of this would help a lot and ensure we maintain quality, and I don't see any issues in these files indicating that we're likely to run into any odd issues in PRs from this (and those would arguably just be surface level and not critically blocking a PR or causing issues, as far as I would guess)

The one thing I wish we could avoid is the space before the compat incs, might be possible some way but I don't think it's a blocker, and compared with the rest of the benefits from this I think it's absolutely a price worth paying for it

TIWAGOS though as I've not evaluated any specific area concerns with include order, but that should probably be caught and we can get some feedback for specific cases

---

TL;DR; I agree with the changes in grouping and I think it helps improve readability and enforcement of this with minimal effort, but can't speak to the practical impact on the code itself

I like the idea of this a LOT, but from past experience with these options I'd say there's room to formalize matters even further. In particular: we should take advantage of `SortPriority`, to allow for ordering within a group without causing spaces. For example, the following would solve the gap issue for `*.compat.inc`:
```diff
   - Regex: ^".*\.compat\.inc"$
-    Priority: 1
+    Priority: 0
+    SortPriority: 1
```
Trying to adjust this even further gets a bit messy, especially if we try to capture with a wildcard after the fact, but I would like to cover our bases as much as possible before fully formalizing anything
Looks great now!
While it could be further adjusted if desired, this is suitable for integration as-is

commits.message: Style: Define clang-format rules for include grouping
Style: Apply clang-format grouping rules

title: Improve thread-safety of `Object` signals
body:
Fixes #117396.

> [!NOTE]
> I would recommend looking at these changes with whitespace changes hidden, as there's a fair bit of indentation changes.

As mentioned in #117396, interacting with signal connections across multiple threads is not currently thread-safe, despite [claims](https://github.com/godotengine/godot/blob/a8e37fc01048823f639c4011af645d2e8e2b79d3/doc/classes/Object.xml#L534) to the contrary. `Object::signal_mutex` is mostly covering `this->signal_map`, but does not cover `target->connections` at all.

This fixes that by replacing the `OBJ_SIGNAL_LOCK` macro and `_ObjectSignalLock` struct with a new `ObjectSignalLock` struct that optionally takes two objects instead of just one. This lets us lock the `signal_mutex` of both objects at the same time, and (more importantly) in the same order every time. The latter is done by simply "sorting" the mutexes by their memory address, since that should be stable for the lifetime of the object.

Most of these changes were fairly simple and mechanical in places were we already had the two objects readily available, but a few were less obvious, so are worth elaborating:

1. `Object::~Object`
    - The scope of the lock when erasing incoming connections has been shrunk to only cover `target->connections`, since we need to lock the `target` mutex. This is technically less thread-safe, since `signal_map` isn't being covered properly, but frankly anything reading/mutating an `Object` currently being destroyed is problematic on so many more levels that this is a non-issue in practice.
    - There was a null check of `c.callable.get_object()` happening during the erasing of outgoing connections, which will always be `this`, so was removed.
2. `Object::_disconnect`
    - There was a null check of `slot->cE` happening before, which is already being done inside of `target_object->connections.erase`, so was removed.
3. `Object::emit_signalp`
    - The scope of the lock has been shrunk to only cover `signal_map` and not the one-shot disconnections. This would otherwise break the lock ordering, since `_disconnect` will internally try to lock both mutexes, but one of them would have (arbitrarily) already been locked.
4. `Object::_remove_user_signal`
    - Since we can't iterate our signal slots while also doing the double-locking, this method needs to be a two-phase thing. We do this by first (under a `this` lock) finding the `slot_map` of the signal, moving it out into a temporary, then erasing it. Then as a second phase we iterate over our now erased `slot_map` and double-lock as per usual.

---

*Disclaimer: AI tooling was used in the planning and review of this change, as well as some minor edits. Any code not physically typed out by myself has been carefully reviewed to the best of my ability.*
author: mihe

reviews.body: I can't think of any situation where this may not work. I guess tests could be added so _tsan_ has a chance to catch them, but I guess that can be done later on.

commits.message: Improve thread-safety of `Object` signals

title: Display and allow setting name/index of BlendSpace points
body: Closes [#12487](https://github.com/godotengine/godot-proposals/issues/12487), closes [#4417](https://github.com/godotengine/godot-proposals/issues/4417), closes [#66336](https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues/66336). ~For ease of review and feedback, this PR's commits are currently separate.~ I'd like to walk through everything this commit adds, so that any additions deemed extraneous can be removed before the squash. ## Allow naming points Points can now be named, and their name will now hold their reference in code. ~This is not a breaking change, all index references remain valid until they are replaced with a name.~ On first launch, indices are transcribed into the string names, that can then be changed by the user. ## Visible index/name In both BlendSpace editors, the index/name is now visible above the point. If this text is too close to the left/right/top edge, it automatically moves itself to fit in the visible view. https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/c2ec8b5b-9c4c-42f3-8c9e-6a4fa84258be The point can also be dragged using its text. ## Unique names upon creation As mentioned in [#12487](https://github.com/godotengine/godot-proposals/issues/12487), points are now given unique names in the same convention as BlendTree and StateMachine. ## Inline editing for names When a point's text is clicked and released without initiating a drag, its name can be edited inline. ~If this name is removed, the point reverts to using its index reference. A purely numeric name results in the forced prefixing of '#'.~ If an empty name is committed, the point reverts to a name based on its node type or animation. https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/3de76906-13ff-4590-93f9-09d5ccac2fbf ## Editing index via toolbar or scrolling directly over points It is possible to edit the index of a point by incrementing/decrementing/specifying a new index via the toolbar, or scrolling over the points. This makes the BlendSpace editor enter a temporary state where indexes are displayed above points, rather than names. ~It is possible edit the name here too, and once again, a purely numeric name results in the forced prefixing of '#'.~ ## Consolidating point editing tools to the right side The toolbar was moved to the right to consolidate all the "point editing" features to one side, to better separate the tools editing the blend space as a whole versus a blend point within it. ~However, since the name box can likely be removed entirely thanks to the inline editing, maybe this change not needed. That being said, I do feel it helps AnimationTree usability as a whole, and can relegate it to another PR.~ *** The issue this PR closes was one of the first major pain points I had with animation in Godot. However, the scope of this is beyond anything I've attempted before, so feedback is very much appreciated. I will integrate recommendations as soon as I can. What in my opinion needs the most rigorous testing is undo/redo, it took me a while to get it free of bugs.
author: vaner-org

reviews.body:
I agree that the current point names are just transcribed array indices and are not meaningful.

~However, in my opinion, regardless of whether these points have meaningful names, we need to implement a way to control the array indices.~

~The reason is that triangle creation priority follows the order in which points are stored in the array. Therefore, this PR should not resolve #66336.~

~I'm still positive about this PR, but adding some kind of GUI that allows users to see the array indices storing points and reorder them (means users should be able to see both the point name and the index of the array) would provide a stronger reason to merge it.~

Ah sorry, I realized I can change the index using the spinner. Please disregard the comment above.

Therefore, it should be fine as long as concern about naming restriction is resolved.

From a usability perspective, changing the index via the spinner is not so intuitive. Then I feel it would be helpful to have a function to move the selected point to either the most top or most bottom (this should allow creating a preferred triangle by applying it any three points in cases where symmetrical quadrilateral occur like https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues/66336).
As I mentioned above, there is no need to allow empty names and fall back. The point of the BlendSpece in old projects should be that they have simple numerical names.

The old code contained two methods: `add_blend_point` and `_add_blend_point`. `add_blend_point` has been modified this time, and a compatibility method has been registered. `_add_blend_point` is being removed because it is an internal method for array serialization and is not publicly exposed due to the leading underscore. Therefore, it should not actually affect compatibility. So you need to add an exclusion for this error under `godot/misc/extension_api_validation/4.6-stable/`.

> Is there an external need for _get_triangles() and _set_triangles() in BlendSpace2D?

If it's not needed immediately, it can be done in a later PR.

> Where should I document the new functionality of rolling the mousewheel to change index, and double clicking the text to change the name?

I think it's better to write it in a tooltip rather than the documentation. FYI, the editor documentation should be sent to https://github.com/godotengine/godot-docs.

I've confirmed that it's working as expected. I'll add the `brakes compat` label just in case, as it involves a minor breaking change; As long as `disable_deprecated` is `false`, it will issue a warning but won't break the project. I'll confirm the final agreement later in the animation meeting.

We discussed this at the animation meeting, but it was difficult to find consensus.

BlendPoint name is StringName, not String. The APIs to `set_blend_point_name` and `get_blend_point_name` and `find_blend_point_by_name` should take StringName.

Tokage notes: The compatibility impact is when the number of points is changed dynamically at runtime, so people who need this can use `get_blend_point_name`

@fire commented during the meeting that this is not something we need, and changing it from an integer to string and having an editable title might be a bad idea.

I and fire agree it may be helpful to have the index number visible at least.

commits.message: Display and allow setting name/index of BlendSpace points

title: Add support for picture-in-picture
body:
This PR adds support for [picture-in-picture](https://developer.android.com/design/ui/mobile/guides/home-screen/picture-in-picture) allowing a Godot application to be moved to a small window pinned to one of the screen corners.
This can be done explicitly via `DisplayServer#pip_mode_enter()` or configured to happen automatically via `DisplayServer#pip_mode_set_auto_enter_on_background(...)` when the application is placed in the background, the latter capability allowing for background playback.

Partially addresses https://github.com/godotengine/godot-proposals/issues/3347 for Android.

**Note:**
Users cannot directly interact with an application in picture-in-picture mode so the feature is most useful for apps and games that have sections that require little to no interactivity. E.g:
- media players
- games with sections showing cinematic contents, i.e: picture-in-picture can be enabled when the game starts showing cinematic contents and disabled when returning to interactive contents


**Example usage**:
Apps can check for the feature availability before explicitly triggering it:
```
func _enter_pip_mode():
    if DisplayServer.has_feature(DisplayServer.FEATURE_PIP_MODE):
        # Configure the aspect ratio, or omit this call to keep the default
        DisplayServer.pip_mode_set_aspect_ratio(1, 1)
        DisplayServer.pip_mode_enter()
```

Apps can configure the system to automatically put them in PiP mode when placed in the background:
```
func _enable_pip_mode_on_background():
    if DisplayServer.has_feature(DisplayServer.FEATURE_PIP_MODE):
        DisplayServer.pip_mode_set_aspect_ratio(16, 9)
        DisplayServer.pip_mode_set_auto_enter_on_background(true)
```

The `NOTIFICATION_APPLICATION_PAUSED` notification is still emitted when the application goes in PiP mode, the app can use `DisplayServer.is_in_pip_mode()` to check whether the app is in PiP mode and not pause its logic. 
The `NOTIFICATION_APPLICATION_PIP_MODE_ENTERED` and `NOTIFICATION_APPLICATION_PIP_MODE_EXITED` can also be used to detect when the application enters / exits PiP mode.
```
func _notification(what):
    if what == NOTIFICATION_APPLICATION_PAUSED:
        if DisplayServer.is_in_pip_mode():
            # Don't pause the logic
        else:
            # Pause the logic

    if what == NOTIFICATION_APPLICATION_PIP_MODE_ENTERED:
        # Update UI for PiP mode
    elif what == NOTIFICATION_APPLICATION_PIP_MODE_EXITED:
        # Revert UI back to normal
```

Here's a recording of a quick integration of the new APIs with the [Rift Riff game](https://play.google.com/store/apps/details?id=com.adriaandejongh.riftriff), where PiP mode is only enabled during one of the game's wave.



https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/ccbcf886-7a6e-4bae-8baa-3636e9c6ca27


<!--
Please target the `master` branch in priority.

Relevant fixes are cherry-picked for stable branches as needed by maintainers.

To speed up the contribution process and avoid CI errors, please set up pre-commit hooks locally:
https://contributing.godotengine.org/en/latest/engine/guidelines/code_style.html
-->

title: Add 3D vertex snap base setting (Vertex/Origin)
body: Follow up to: * https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/117235 Adds `Vertex Snap Base` section to the 3D editor transform menu. `Vertex` mode enables the user to select a vertex from a source node(s) with geometry and snap that vertex to a target one. `Origin` will simply snap the selected node(s) origin to the target vertex. **Note:** Nodes without geometry such as `Camera3D` or `Marker3D` always use `Origin` (this was the case in the previous PR as well). Addresses: https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/117235#issuecomment-4063691743 Demo: https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/52050c90-d614-4f0b-b479-739ba9fec638
author: ryevdokimov

reviews.body: Tested locally, it works as expected. Code looks good to me. In terms of UX, this seems fine but I think the Transform menu should be changed to be more consistent with the View menu. Currently, we rarely (if ever) use separators above sections of radio buttons in the editor. Current | Proposed -|- | There should also be tooltips for each option: - **Snap Vertex to Vertex:** - Hold (shortcut key) to highlight a vertex on the currently selected node, then drag the mouse to move the node and snap it to neighboring nodes' vertices. For nodes that do not have a vertex-based representation, Snap Origin to Vertex is always used instead. - **Snap Origin to Vertex:** - Hold (shortcut key) to highlight another node's vertex, then click to teleport the selected node to the highlighted vertex.
Works great now 🙂

title: OpenXR: Add support for `XR_KHR_generic_controller`
body:
This PR adds support for the new [XR_KHR_generic_controller](https://registry.khronos.org/OpenXR/specs/1.1/html/xrspec.html#XR_KHR_generic_controller) extension that became available in OpenXR 1.1.52 (#110777)

This is a new generic controller profile added to OpenXR that is represents the norm in XR controllers and should provide a suitable fallback where the "simple controller" profile is insufficient.

Marking this as a draft PR for now as we're awaiting confirmation which runtimes have added support for this extension.

Once enough runtimes support this new controller profile we'll have to have a discussion whether we'll restrict our default action map to this profile alongside the hand interaction profile.
author: BastiaanOlij

reviews.body: Thanks! This looks good to me :-)

commits.message: OpenXR: Add support for XR_KHR_generic_controller

title: Misc includes or modernize fixes found via clangd-tidy
body:
- Helps address https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues/111218.
- Follow-up to #117286.
- Follow-up to #117295.
- Follow-up to #117301.
- Follow-up to #117328.
- Follow-up to #117366.
- Follow-up to #117402.

After the series of PRs dealing with each section of the engine (minus some Apple platform code I can't analyze from Linux), I did another pass to see if there were any stragglers I missed or omitted from previous PRs.

This PR handles not only `unused-includes` but also a few `modernize-*` checks that we have configured in `.clang-tidy`, which `clangd-tidy` (note the `d`) also reads.

I ran most checks for the Linux platform with Clang 21 on Fedora 43, but I also checked Android, Windows, and Web specific files with a `compile_commands.json` for these platforms.

See [this thread](https://chat.godotengine.org/channel/yQRJDrPTpn5ijkZFt?msg=32QpvZddMQkEG6gMn) for tips on configuring for Emscripten, and [this thread](https://chat.godotengine.org/channel/yQRJDrPTpn5ijkZFt?msg=fCEbCy9xRuDDoyBNe) for my failed attempt at using clangd with osxcross for macOS.

These changes should pave the way to integrating `clangd-tidy` on CI, at least on a subset of the codebase initially. On my system, the non platform-specific files are now diagnostic free for our current config.

Quick notes on which paths require specific handling as they're platform-specific:
```
drivers/{alsa,alsamidi,apple*,core*,d3d12,metal,pulseaudio,wasapi,windows,winmidi,xaudio2}/
editor/shaders/shader_baker/shader_baker_export_plugin_platform_{d3d12,metal}.*
modules/camera/camera_{android,feed_linux,linux,macos,win}.*
modules/openxr/extensions/platform/openxr_{android,metal}_extension.*
platform/{name}/* minus platform/{name}/{api,export}/*
```
author: akien-mga

reviews.body: From a purely include perspective, looks good to me.
I'm missing context for a handful of include / library related changes, but the rest look good to me and I trust Akien with the rest :)

commits.message: Misc includes or modernize fixes found via clangd-tidy

title: [LinuxBSD] Add support for HDR output (Wayland)
body:
Description updated for 2026/02/23.

Based on https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/94496 (merged to master)
Implements https://github.com/godotengine/godot-proposals/issues/10817 for Wayland
Testing/Sample project: https://github.com/DarkKilauea/godot-hdr-output

This PR implements the luminance based features of the [wp-color-management](https://wayland.app/protocols/color-management-v1) protocol as well as the minimum amount of colorspace + transfer function features necessary for HDR output.

Due to the unreliability of HDR applying glare compensation or not, SDR over HDR is assumed to be performed against a monitor which does not apply glare compensation which differs from the assumptions made in the Windows and Mac implementations for HDR. The Wayland protocol was made with this assumption so we will not deviate from it despite having the possibility to do so.

Since this feature is exclusive to Wayland, the display server must be set to Wayland in both project settings and editor settings (not setting both to Wayland leads to inconsistencies in display server selection).

For editor: `run/platforms/linuxbsd/prefer_wayland` must be enabled.
For project: `display/display_server/driver.linuxbsd` set to "wayland" (X11 is still set chosen when "deafault" is selected).
author: ArchercatNEO

reviews.body:
Tested locally, I can't see any difference when toggling HDR, even after restarting the editor. Changing the luminance project settings also doesn't make a visible difference (HDR 2D is enabled in the project settings too). I used the same test project as https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/94496#pullrequestreview-3007094400.

HDR is enabled in the KDE system settings, and I see a difference on the rest of the screen.

<details open>

<summary>PC specifications</summary>

- **CPU:** AMD Ryzen 9 9950X3D
- **GPU:** NVIDIA GeForce RTX 5090
- **RAM:** 64 GB (2×32 GB DDR5-6000 CL30)
- **SSD:** Solidigm P44 Pro 2 TB
- **OS:** Fedora 42
- **Monitor:** ASUS ROG PG32UCDP
</details>

```
Operating System: Fedora Linux 42
KDE Plasma Version: 6.4.1
KDE Frameworks Version: 6.15.0
Qt Version: 6.9.1
Kernel Version: 6.15.4-200.fc42.x86_64 (64-bit)
Graphics Platform: Wayland
```

Using NVIDIA 575.64 with the open kernel driver.

I finally got access to a laptop running Fedora 43 and was able to test this PR out. Aside from a few minor issues, it seems to work rather well on both my internal and external display.

I did notice that GNOME on my system doesn't seem to support HDR, in that it doesn't provide color primaries through the Wayland interface, despite having an "HDR" checkbox in the settings. KDE works great however and the EDR feature for SDR screens is pretty cool.

I left a few comments on some things I noticed that should be addressed. Thanks for all the effort put into this so far!

Tested locally on https://github.com/DarkKilauea/godot-hdr-output (rebased on top of `master` https://github.com/godotengine/godot/commit/bf95b62586e31b8a3503f5903d7764d7c52bf2ab), it works as expected. Code looks good to me.

This time, it's working well on NVIDIA with no special layers installed.

<details open>

<summary>PC specifications</summary>

- **CPU:** AMD Ryzen 9 9950X3D
- **GPU:** NVIDIA GeForce RTX 5090 (driver 580.119.02)
- **RAM:** 64 GB (2×32 GB DDR5-6000 CL30)
- **SSD:** Solidigm P44 Pro 2 TB
- **OS:** Linux (Fedora 43)
</details>

```
Operating System: Fedora Linux 43
KDE Plasma Version: 6.5.5
KDE Frameworks Version: 6.22.0
Qt Version: 6.10.1
Kernel Version: 6.18.8-200.fc43.x86_64 (64-bit)
Graphics Platform: Wayland
```

Some notes:

- Custom reference and max luminance are not supported. Values are always read from the system settings.

```
ERROR: DisplayServer does not support setting a manual reference luminance
   at: window_set_hdr_output_reference_luminance (platform/linuxbsd/wayland/display_server_wayland.cpp:1537)
   GDScript backtrace (most recent call first):
       [0] _on_custom_reference_luminance_toggled (res://hdr_controls.gd:85)
ERROR: DisplayServer does not support setting a manual maximum luminance
   at: window_set_hdr_output_max_luminance (platform/linuxbsd/wayland/display_server_wayland.cpp:1555)
   GDScript backtrace (most recent call first):
       [0] _on_custom_max_luminance_toggled (res://hdr_controls.gd:96)
```

- This is printed when starting the HDR demo:

```
WARNING: HDR output requested but no HDR compatible format was found, falling back to SDR.
     at: _determine_swap_chain_format (drivers/vulkan/rendering_device_driver_vulkan.cpp:3506)
```

However, HDR output clearly appears to be functional (and looks correct, with only highlights becoming brighter and more detailed).

- HDR output does not work correctly when using llvmpipe (`--gpu-index 1`). You can enable HDR, but everything will look washed out, almost as

I know basically nothing about HDR so I did a review of the Wayland-specific parts. Just a few nitpicks and notes before I approve, but overall this looks awesome!

(The `Ref<T>` comment was meant to be part of this review, sorry 😅 )
The Wayland side of things looks great!
Code looks good. I was not able to test it, my Fedora install refuse to detect HDR monitor (which works fine on Windows on the same system).

I'm ok with the current state of this PR. 

We'll have to make some changes in the Android PR if this gets merged before that to address setting the RDD::Colorspace correctly. It might be worth having a follow up PR after all of the HDR PRs are merged to streamline the selection logic for the format and color space.

title: Poison `CowData` pointer during destruction in sanitizer builds.
body:
- Related to https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/105146/
    - In particular, this PR is sanitizer-build only, but it also finds improper `read` instead of just improper `write`. I think both PRs are warranted.

Finally! I have found a way to sanely track down https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues/101830.
The builds currently fail for `master` because the asan builds are correctly detecting misuse of `CowData`. We're going to need to address the issue before we can merge this PR.

The way this works is to 'poison' `CowData` while it destructs. This makes sanitizers complain when the destructing `CowData` is accessed during destruction. 
`CowData` (like all other objects) should not be accessed during destruction because it may be in a half-destructed state (e.g. parts of the objects already deallocated).
author: Ivorforce

commits.message: Poison `CowData` pointer during destruction in sanitizer builds.

title: Remove unused includes in `modules` with clangd-tidy
body:
- Helps address https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues/111218.
- Follow-up to #117286.
- Follow-up to #117295.
- Follow-up to #117301.
- Follow-up to #117328.
- Follow-up to #117366.

~I left out Jolt which @mihe will take a look at - its thirdparty includes should use angled brackets to remove warnings, and then there's a few actually unused/not directly used headers that can be cleaned up.~ *Edit:* Added in the end as it was trivial.

I should have done the same for the OpenXR module as it turned out pretty hairy. `openxr_platform_inc.h` is horrifying, pulling a ton of platform specific stuff in headers when most of the time it just needs `vulkan.h` or some Metal header. I strongly recommend refactoring this further and decoupling OpenXR stuff as much as possible from platform-specific includes in headers. There also seems to be a pattern of including `openxr_api.h` and `openxr_util.h` everywhere even when not needed just to get `<openxr/openxr.h>` or a few other dependencies. These things add up and likely make recompile times pretty bad for folks working on OpenXR, so I advise you look into reducing the use of this pattern.
author: akien-mga

reviews.body: The ability for modules to use relative includes certainly made this a bit tougher to parse, but everything appears to be in order

commits.message: Remove unused includes in `modules` with clangd-tidy

Jolt: Use angled brackets for thirdparty includes

That's how we usually include thirdparty code, and it solves warnings
from clangd/IWYU about some Jolt headers (notably `Jolt/Jolt.h`) not being
used directly but as proxies to transitive includes.

title: macOS: Work around AppKit include defining `MAX`/`MIN` macros
body:
Compiling Godot on a MacOS could produce a header conflict where MacOS runtime MAX macro was used instead Core/Typedefs MAX macro

Example: Defining a FixedVector in a header file would fail, due to MacOS MAX not being a ConstEXPR
author: ColinSORourke

commits.message:
Catch Header Conflict for Macs

Compiling Godot on a MacOS could produce a header conflict where MacOS runtime MAX macro was used instead Core/Typedefs MAX macro

Example: Defining a FixedVector in a header file would fail, due to MacOS MAX not being a ConstEXPR

title: Clean up includes in `drivers/d3d12`
body:
- Extracted from #117328.

While working on the above PR I took the chance to refactor the `drivers/d3d12` code a bit to wrap some thirdparty headers that require specific pragmas or definitions, which adds `godot_d3dx12.h`, `godot_d3d12ma.h`, and `godot_nir.h`. These new wrapper headers should be included with angled brackets to be properly treated as "system"/thirdparty, which avoids some diagnostics to show up with clangd.

I also cleaned up the pragmas to reduce them to what seems to be the minimal changes to compile (possibly some of these warnings were solved in D3D12/Mesa). Needs testing, especially with MSVC as I only tested mingw-gcc and llvm-mingw from Linux.

I removed the `AS` typedef for AudioServer as we don't use it and it caused a `-Wshadow` warning in one of the thirdparty D3D12 headers.
author: akien-mga

reviews.body: Tested on MSVC, compilation failed in `nir.h`. Looks like the other suppression *is* still required, though I'm uncertain why it didn't show up on CI
Everything now builds as expected
Seems to be building and running fine.

commits.message: Clean up includes in `drivers/d3d12`

title: Fix error in debugger while changing scene on button clicked with touchscreen
body:
When changing scenes via button click with a touchscreen, nodes can be removed from the tree during input event processing. The ERR_FAIL_COND_V assertion in can_process() would trigger an error when called on nodes already removed from the tree. Changed to return false instead of asserting, which is the correct behavior for nodes not in the tree.

I modified Node::canprocess in node.cpp to return false when a node isn't in a tree rather than trying to assert it. 

This should fix #115376
author: CoolPartyMann

reviews.body:
Discussed in the core meeting. Since this function is exposed, we think it's better to silently return `false`.
Just ATS' comment should be addressed.

commits.message:
Fix error in debugger while changing scene on button clicked with touchscreen

When changing scenes via button click with a touchscreen, nodes can be removed
from the tree during input event processing. The ERR_FAIL_COND_V assertion in
can_process() would trigger an error when called on nodes already removed from
the tree. Changed to return false instead of asserting, which is the correct
behavior for nodes not in the tree.

Fixes #115376

title: Add freelook and navigation control to preview camera
body: Closes: https://github.com/godotengine/godot-proposals/issues/3297 Supersedes: https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/100875 ~~Requires and Includes: https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/117180~~ (merged) This PR enables freelook and navigation control (middle mouse panning/orbiting/zooming) via a checkbox made visible after enabling preview: Demo: https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/434c00f3-1e6e-4e69-8159-dc9fa224b333
author: ryevdokimov

reviews.body:
Tested, the overall UX of the feature makes sense.

It currently has problems related to mismatch of camera position and cursor:

1. When you first start orbiting with middle mouse button it snaps to a wrong angle first.
2. If you animate the camera with an AnimationPlayer (a common use for piloting) and try to pilot the camera after scrubbing through the timeline it will snap to a wrong position, ignoring the actual camera position that the animation player has set.

I also noticed that having to always click two checkboxes when i.e trying to animate two separate cameras quickly gets old. I suggest considering these improvements:

1. If we're in a camera preview mode, selecting another camera in the scene tree should switch the viewport to the other camera (if we only have one viewport), preserving the state of the piloting checkbox.
2. Maybe there could be a power feature where Shift+clicking the preview checkbox would also enable piloting + a mention of that behavior in the tooltip to make it easier to discover.
Tested, can confirm that the unrelated commit fixes the issue. Also all of the other bugs that I found have been fixed. Approving on UX, this still needs a code review.

commits.message: Add freelook and navigation control to preview camera

title: Fix "Send Key(s) to RESET" not appearing on first right-click in Animation Editor
body:
Fixes #117100.

The cause of issue is that `selection`(the selected keys) is updated next frame due to a deferred signal connection (`CONNECT_DEFERRED`), but `AnimationTrackEditor::can_add_reset_key` relis on its value at this frame. This commit extracts the menu pop up logic as a single function and uses `call_deferred` to call it to use the updated `selection`.

For context, `selection` is updated by `_try_select_at_ui_pos` -> `emit_signal(SNAME("select_key"), key_idx, true)` -> `AnimationTrackEditor::_key_selected` and is used in `AnimationTrackEditor::can_add_reset_key`.
author: rinevard

title: Add vertex snapping to the 3D editor
body:
<!--
Please target the `master` branch. We will take care of backporting relevant fixes to older versions.

Before submitting, please read our checklist for new contributors:
https://contributing.godotengine.org/en/latest/engine/introduction.html#checklist-for-new-contributors
-->
 

* Closes: https://github.com/godotengine/godot-proposals/issues/755

Hold the `Vertex Snap` shortcut (default: `B`) to highlight the nearest vertex on the selected node's geometry. Click and drag to snap that vertex to vertices on other meshes. For nodes without geometry (`Camera3D`, `Marker3D`, etc.), the node's origin snaps directly to the target vertex.

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/efa0c33d-d6dc-4b50-9114-0b184c18586d
author: ryevdokimov

reviews.body: Tested locally with a mix of CSG nodes, MeshInstance3Ds, Decals and Marker3Ds, it works as expected. Some feedback: - It's possible to enable vertex snapping while dragging gizmos or using Blender-style manipulation shortcuts. However, it has no visible effect other than drawing the yellow circle gizmo around a vertex. - It should be possible to release B after starting a dragging operation, so you don't have to keep it held down the whole way. Right now, releasing B while dragging will stop moving the selected node (but won't cancel the operation). - The vertex snap gizmo could be drawn twice (once opaque with normal depth, once translucent with depth test disabled), so that you can be aware that you're snapping to a vertex that is occluded by another object: Current | Proposed (mockup) -|- | We use the same trick for the 3D selection box, so it appears translucent when occluded. - Non-geometry nodes like Decal, ReflectionProbe and Marker3D behave a bit oddly. You can enable vertex snapping and move the node according to the snapped vertex, but the node won't center around the vertex like one would probably expect. Instead, it will move relative to your mouse movement, taking the snapped vertex into account. - I can't think of a lot of use cases for this, so I think it would make more sense to move the node's origin to the snapped vertex instead. This makes sense for Marker3D nodes in particular, which may be used as "shot origins" on weapon models, characters, etc. - Out of curiosity, is edge/face snapping planned in the future?

Works great now 🙂 

Some more feedback:

- The gizmo will display at the wrong position until you move the mouse when teleporting a Marker3D node through vertex snapping:

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/1296d6af-a238-46b6-9b3c-5a969cfdc91c

- The gizmo displays at a very low opacity when occluded. I'd increase it a little, as it's hard to see right now, especially behind complex geometry or textures.

I did some more testing with nested node trees, instanced scenes, etc. I've also checked the vertex snap behavior at various editor scales and it behaves as expected.

I've also tested vertex snapping in complex scenes like https://github.com/Calinou/godot-reflection and performance seemed OK. I do wonder if there's room for improvement on performance still, as dragging a mesh with vertex snapping is clearly less smooth the more complex the scene gets.

However, on https://github.com/godotengine/tps-demo, it gets very slow when using vertex snap because the scene geometry is pretty complex (less than 1 FPS). We should probably have an option to snap using collision data instead of visual mesh data, as it's much simpler in this scene. Maybe we should do this automatically for meshes that are under a PhysicsBody3D parent?

Code looks good to me.

Works great now. The performance level is much more practical on the TPS demo as well as Reflection now, at least when using an optimized editor build (`production=yes lto=full`). On a debug build with `optimize=none`, it's still pretty slow on the TPS demo, but it's usable on Reflection.

I noticed that using vertex snap to move a node does not reset the follow mode (like https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/117289 did for other transforms). It should do that as well. Perhaps starting a vertex snap operation while in follow mode should cancel the follow mode too, as it often behaves erratically due to the sudden teleportation.
Code and functionality seem good.

commits.message: Add vertex snapping to the 3D editor

title: GDScript: Improve evaluation of constant expressions with arrays/dictionaries
body:
> [!IMPORTANT]
> This PR also fixes a bug (eliminates an inconsistency between `Array` and packed arrays) that some users may consider a breaking change, see the [comment](#issuecomment-3737468559) below.

* Fixes #81256.
* Fixes #95568.
* Fixes #95851.
* Fixes #96152.
* Fixes #103200.
* Fixes #113278.
* Fixes #115428.

I've noticed that users are complaining that the following code doesn't work:

```gdscript
@export_custom(
    PROPERTY_HINT_TYPE_STRING,
    "%d/%d:Zero,One,Two" % [TYPE_INT, PROPERTY_HINT_ENUM],
)
var my_array: Array
```

due to the error `Argument 2 of annotation "@export_custom" isn't a constant expression.`.

As a workaround, you can use:

```gdscript
const FORMAT_PARAMS = [TYPE_INT, PROPERTY_HINT_ENUM]
@export_custom(
    PROPERTY_HINT_TYPE_STRING,
    "%d/%d:Zero,One,Two" % FORMAT_PARAMS,
)
var my_array: Array
```

But this is not the most intuitive solution, and it also requires you to have a redundant constant.

The main problem is that our main expression reduction mechanism doesn't take context into account (typed context, constant context), and array/dictionary "literals" (`[]` and `{}` constructs) create untyped, non-constant arrays/dictionaries by default. While changing this system requires relatively significant effort, fixing constant reduction within the existing `make_*_reduced_value()` helper functions is fairly straightforward.
author: dalexeev

reviews.body:
LGTM. If the change to regular constant folding has any unwanted effects we will find out I guess.

I don't think the "compat break" with packed arrays is of much concern. Since there was never a writeback for methods like `append` it was no way to monitor array changes reliable anyway. So I don't see any usecase for the old behavior.

Core changes look good. Packed arrays are indeed shared inside `Variant`.

However, I think the history of the function should be considered before merge:

- The function started in GODOT IS OPEN SOURCE without considering packed arrays as shared.
- Later, @vnen changed this via https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/62462/, to fix setter related bugs.
- Later still, @vnen reverted this change via https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/72398 because packed arrays "are copied on C++/script boundaries".

Indeed, packed arrays behave differently inside and outside `Variant`. For example, `Variant` based calls copy the arrays across the boundary, while the `ptrcall` does not cause a copy to be made.
I still think `is_type_shared` makes sense to be `true` for packed arrays in the context of `Variant`, but we should tread carefully to not overinterpret this information.
I checked `is_type_shared` and `is_shared`, and they seem to only be used inside GDScript / the GDScript vm. 

So please confirm you're confident the GDScript changes are fine, especially with regards to https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/72398 / https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues/62857. If so, I agree we should merge this.

commits.message: GDScript: Improve evaluation of constant expressions with arrays/dictionaries

title: Remove arbitrary limits on BlendSpace editors' visible region
body:
BlendSpace1D and BlendSpace2D's editors do not allow the min value of their space to be greater than 0, and the max value to be less than 0.01. However, `animation_blend_space_1d.cpp` and `animation_blend_space_2d.cpp` only enforce that the min limit be less than the max limit.

Considering the user's ability to change `value` in BlendSpace1D, and `x` and `y` in BlendSpace2D to anything, one assumes that they should be able to map these BlendSpaces' visible area 1:1 to the range of the specific variable that controls them in their code, even if that range is all negative.

Safeguarding the visibility of 0 so that the user doesn't get disoriented seems to me to be the only reason for this, but that can be rectified by a future PR that adds numbering to grid divisions as well.

Additionally, grid lines are never drawn when at the max edge, that is also fixed here.
author: vaner-org

reviews.body: I think the intended purpose is fine.

commits.message: Remove arbitrary limits on BlendSpace editors' visible region

title: Draw aggregate animation keys on top of folded node groups
body:
Relates to godotengine/godot-proposals#1908.
Complements #113479.

This changes the drawing of folded node groups in the animation editor (as introduced by #113479) to show all keys from all tracks, but at a 30% opacity, giving you a summary overview of all the node's tracks.

Note that key links will not be drawn, nor are keys necessarily drawn the way they would be drawn in the actual track, like for example animation playback tracks and audio playback tracks. We felt that this would make things look too noisy, so keys are simply drawn as their respective track type icons.

Also note that the keys cannot be selected nor edited, they are simply drawn on top of the collapsed group.

title: Move enum registration from `ClassDB` / `ClassInfo` to `GDType`.
body:
- Follow-up of #106016 
- Works towards https://github.com/godotengine/godot-proposals/issues/12323

Now it's starting to get interesting :)
This PR is the first step towards replacing `ClassDB` (for the purposes of type capabilities), as proposed in https://github.com/godotengine/godot-proposals/issues/12323.

### Explanation

The PR makes a few important decisions:
- `GDType` gets `init_state` which protects against changes after the type gets its first subtype. This _should be_ compatible with all GDExtensions, at least if they use the extension APIs as expected (with something like Godot's own `_bind_methods` called immediately after registration).
- `constant_map` and `enum_map` are cached (like `name_hierarchy`) from constants and enums across all supertypes. This avoids the expensive walk through parents on lookup that `ClassDB` is doing in `master`.
    - `self_constant_map` and `self_enum_map` keep track of the declarations on the type itself, which is also sometimes relevant.
- All maps are exposed as their actual type (`const AHashMap &`). `ClassDB` opted for a more verbose function-based interface, which I think is reinventing the wheel for interacting with hash maps. Internally, exposing the hash maps should give us more powerful tools for introspection.
- `EnumInfo` is exposed, for similar reasons.
- `initialize_class` is slightly better protected against multi-threaded shenanigans (with `BinaryMutex`). We don't expect more threads to access it, but it won't hurt to be sure about it.
- `constant_order` is removed. It only existed in `DEBUG` (making the builds differ in behavior) and was duplicating entries in the return `List`.
author: Ivorforce

reviews.body: Tested locally on several demo projects (and DOGWALK), it works as expected. Code looks good to me.

Thanks!

The code looks good to me. I did a little bit of testing as well, comparing the `extension_api.json` before/after this change, and running godot-cpp tests. That all looked good!

However, I tried on opening a random real project in the editor, and got some new error spam - the specific one pointed out below

Thanks!

I re-tested this with the godot-cpp tests, and opening in the editor (and running) the demos for two GDExtensions that I maintain. Didn't have any issues!

I didn't look at the code as closely this time as I did on my previous review, but it looks fine to me on a quick read through the diff.

This needs a rebase and there's one code style thing with a comment that ATS pointed out that's unaddressed, but otherwise, looks good to me :-)

Create caches across supertypes for faster lookup.

title: Add `has_script_method` to `Script` for scripting access
body:
This addresses a longstanding TODO around the use of `Script::has_method` versus `Object::has_method`.

In the `Script` class, the method `has_method` was introduced as a way to identify whether a specific script declares a specific method, since `get_script_method_list` returns all methods from the given script instance up through all its non-native ancestors' script classes. 

On the Engine side, this reimplementation does not cause any issues; however, it presents an issue from GDExtension, as the call to `Script::has_method` delegates to the `Object::has_method` binding, and not the virtual redeclaration of the same method on `Script`.

With the closing of 4.6 and 4.7 around the corner, it made sense to go ahead and address this. This PR aligns both `has_method` and `has_static_method` methods on the `Script` class with the _script_ moniker prefix, aligning them with other methods like `has_script_signal`.

With this change, GDExtension code can now call `Script::has_script_method` on a GDScript instance, and it will invoke the `GScript::has_script_method` call rather than delegating to `Object` as it did previously.
author: Naros

reviews.body: Looks good to me. :+1:
Seems like basic functionality that is needed for GDExtensions that work with scripts.

commits.message: Add `has_script_method` to `Script` for scripting access

title: Use Grisu2 algorithm in `String::num_scientific` to fix serializing
body:
Supersedes PR https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/96676, PR https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/86951, and fixes https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues/78204, fixes #82424, fixes #99103, fixes #99763, see also https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/100414.

This PR replaces the algorithm in `String::num_scientific` with Grisu2 to serialize numbers with more precision. The implementation was copied from simdjson here: https://github.com/simdjson/simdjson/blob/master/src/to_chars.cpp and adjusted slightly to match the existing behavior of `String::num_scientific`.

What: Grisu2 is an algorithm for serializing floats in scientific notation, with enough precision to ensure they can be read back exactly, while also having the minimum amount of digits, ensuring compactness and human readability. It uses integer operations and a table of pre-computed powers of ten, so it is extremely fast.

Why: We need to serialize with more precision to ensure that a serialized number can be deserialized into the same number. For example, for the number `123456789`, the closest 32-bit float is `123456792`. In master this is serialized as `1.23457e8`, which becomes `123457000`, over 200 off from the closest 32-bit float. With this PR, if a 32-bit float, it will be serialized with 9 digits as `123456790`, which can be read back as exactly `123456792`. 32-bit floats have 6 reliable digits, but up to 9 are needed to serialize to decimal in order to read back with full precision.

For an example with 64-bit floats, I have `1.234567898765432123456789e30` included in the test cases. The closest 64-bit float is `1.23456789876543218850569440461e30` (differs at the 8 which used to be a 2). This gets serialized as `1.2345678987654322e+30` which is deserialized to exactly `1.23456789876543218850569440461e30`. 64-bit floats have 14 reliable digits, but up to 17 are needed to serialize to decimal in order to read back with full precision.

Note that the code in Variant writer for Vector2/Vector3/etc has been adjusted to work with both 32-bit and 64-bit floats, so it will correctly serialize the numbers for builds with either precision level.

Note that the docs have special code that always use the 32-bit version, since we don't need high precision in the docs.

Note that I kept the existing behavior where `num_scientific` does not have a trailing `.0`, but the code I grabbed from simdjson included that, so I removed it. It would be easy to add that back in. However I also separately re-added the trailing `.0` for the documentation to ensure the docs are generated with `.0` like before.
author: aaronfranke

reviews.body:
I'm definitely in favor of using the same code for float serialization/print, and the implementation looks good. `sprintf` is too implementation dependent and unreliable.

> I was curious why you renamed rtos_fixed to serialize_real.

It's internal method, so doesn't matter. But I like `serialize_real` more.
This is a good proposal. Would like more consensus.

Tested locally on Linux on the [3D Platformer](https://github.com/godotengine/godot-demo-projects/tree/master/3d/platformer) demo, this causes issues with floats being saved with too much precision, therefore causing unnecessary VCS diffs:

```diff

index 31e33324..ddfa0aac 100644

+++ b/3d/platformer/coin/coin.tscn
@@ -6,9 +6,9 @@

-top_radius = 0.2
-bottom_radius = 0.2
-height = 0.05
+top_radius = 0.20000000298023224
+bottom_radius = 0.20000000298023224
+height = 0.05000000074505806
 radial_segments = 16
 rings = 1
 
@@ -33,10 +33,10 @@ albedo_color = Color(1, 0.858824, 0.572549, 0.25098)
 albedo_texture = SubResource("GradientTexture2D_qhu5r")
 billboard_mode = 1

This is a great idea, we should have a very robust float printing system.

I'd like to know, is there any particular reason you chose `simdjson` for the implementation?
The algorithm looks good at a glance, but it makes some decisions for us that we might want to make ourselves - for example, when to use `e` notation over plain floating point. (I guess we could patch it, if we wanted to make a decision?). It may also be nice to print directly into a `char32_t` buffer, although the speed difference probably won't matter all too much.
More interestingly, simdjson does not expose a way to _not_ do scientific notation (although it should be easy to patch this in). I'd argue that if we were to merge this, we should use `grisu2` for `String::num` as well.

Looks good to me overall.

A bit concerned about the impact this may have on user projects when it comes to git noise on upgrade. We'll want to make sure users get a suggestion to run the tool we have to re-save all scenes to apply those changes all at once. (CC @KoBeWi )

title: GDScript: Fix and simplify coroutine stack clearing
body:
* Fixes #117049.
* Fixes #117164.
* Regression from #116711.

This PR simplifies coroutine stack clearing. When a coroutine resumed, the stack was previously stored in multiple `GDScriptFunctionState`s (via the `Ref<GDScriptFunctionState> first_state;` field).

Now, previous `GDScriptFunctionState`s are no longer held in memory. After a suspended function resumes, responsibility for the stack passes to `GDScriptFunction::call()`, which cleans it up at the end as usual.

The `first_state` field was introduced in #19828. I made sure that this change doesn't reintroduce #19823:

```gdscript
extends Node

func _ready():
    while true:
        await get_tree().process_frame
        await get_tree().process_frame
        await get_tree().process_frame
        var a: PackedByteArray
        var b := RefCounted.new()
        print("await 3 idle frames ", Performance.get_monitor(Performance.OBJECT_COUNT))
```

Before, it was `previous_state`, introduced in #17291. I made sure that this change doesn't reintroduce #17280:

```gdscript
extends Node

func _ready():
    await test()
    print("ready is over")

func test():
    await $Button.pressed
    await $Button2.pressed
```
author: dalexeev

commits.message: GDScript: Fix and simplify coroutine stack clearing

title: Fix TabContainer set current tab not immediately updating visibility
body:
Fix TabContainer set current tab not immediately updating visibility
- Fixes https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues/99065
- Fixes https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues/115947
- Supersedes https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/116119

`_on_tab_changed` repaint was made deferred back in #58687, probably because repaint uses `theme_cache` which will crash if used too early. But now `TabContainer::set_current_tab` doesn't immediately set the current tab, it waits for `NOTIFICATION_ENTER_TREE`, since #83893.
This means it should be safe to repaint immediately when the tab changes.

I tested and didn't find any issues with it.

This also fixes the AnimationTree dock issue, because the TabContainer not being visible immediately caused this check to not work:

https://github.com/godotengine/godot/blob/220b0b2f74d8e089481b140c42a42992a76dd6fc/editor/animation/animation_player_editor_plugin.cpp#L2490-L2491

Previously it was only possible to have the AnimationTree dock open automatically if the AnimationPlayer dock was already open. This was because this check in the dock manger never changed the tab to the AnimationPlayer dock because it detected that it was already visible since it didn't update yet to show the tree:

https://github.com/godotengine/godot/blob/220b0b2f74d8e089481b140c42a42992a76dd6fc/editor/docks/editor_dock_manager.cpp#L663

Before that was a lot of dock manager changes, I haven't checked what made it work in 4.5.

`EditorData::get_handling_sub_editors` should probably be not in reverse order too, that way user plugins (and more specific plugins like AnimationTree) take priority over editor plugins, since they are opened in order and the last opened will override the others.
This was sort of even the goal with the PR that changed it #37022
author: kitbdev

commits.message: Fix TabContainer set current tab not immediately updating visibility

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/4b594a16-852b-4dee-b1b0-ed8d1c4d7d4b

- When using relative positioning, CSG gizmos don't update until you move your mouse over one of the handles:

To test this, use the MRP I posted in https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/116749, move the ground away from the house and scale it non-uniformly with parent transforms enabled using this PR.

title: Add finite check for `normalize()` in vector classes
body: - Supersedes/closes https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/116422 - Fixes https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues/73299 Add a finite check to the `normalize()` function. In practice, cases occur where `normalize()` does not produce a normalized result when the vector length is zero. Currently, it returns the zero vector in such cases. Since many implementations in Godot's core rely on this zero vector being returned, adding a check for the zero vector would cause a large number of errors to be spammed and break compatibility. Therefore, I will limit the change to adding only the finite check. Also, the addition checks were kept to a minimum since they affect performance. Specifically, in the case of Script https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues/73299, the warning occurs due to using `Vector3()::normalize()` instead of `Basis::orthonormalize()`. However, since what's argument passed to the function is a Transform3D, I don't think it makes much difference whether it's a Basis warning or a Vector3 warning, although the confusion with the origin and the basis elements possibly occur.. In any case, since now it can warn that something within Transform3D is infinite, this is far preferable to the original warning. By the way, I remember we briefly discussed in the past the possibility of improving `interpolate_with()` to avoid using `orthonormalized()` altogether in https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/72287. While this should be a completely separate PR, if feasible, it could potentially eliminate from `interpolate_with()` entirely (Although the result is expected to be either nan or infinite, so another function will likely throw an error).
author: TokageItLab

reviews.body:
Reviewed in animation meeting and we think it looks good.

It technically breaks compat if a user performs a normalize on non-finite, but I think this is acceptable.

As ever with these kind of checks, it's probably also worth considering whether to have `WARN_PRINT` or `WARN_PRINT_ONCE` here, as if you have 1 bad plane, you've probably got 1000. Although if the error toaster can combine these errors efficiently it's not so bad.

I guess that is the kind of problem that will show up with use though.

commits.message: Add finite check for normalize() in vector classes

title: Fix `RenderingDevice::texture_clear()` behaving unexpectedly when using the D3D12 driver
body: Fixes: https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues/117210 Using `RenderingDevice::texture_clear()` led to different results between the Vulkan and D3D12 driver, depending on the `DataFormat` of the texture. This PR changes the D3D12 driver to use `ID3D12GraphicsCommandList::ClearUnorderedAccessViewFloat()` for clearing color textures instead of `ID3D12GraphicsCommandList::ClearUnorderedAccessViewUint()`. As far as my testing goes, this should make the result of `RenderingDevice::texture_clear()` be on par between the D3D12 and Vulkan driver. The following example clears textures with different data formats to `Color.HOT_PINK` (left to right: `R8G8B8A8_UNORM`, `R32G32B32A32_SFLOAT`, `R8_UNORM`, `R32_SFLOAT`). **Before:** **This PR:** Example project: [texture-clear-example.zip](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/25876690/texture-clear-example.zip) From what I've read about the behavior of those two clearing functions, I think that `ClearUnorderedAccessViewFloat()` would be more appropriate for clearing color textures. However, I am not sure whether I am missing something here that might cause issues, as I don't know whether there was a reason for using `ClearUnorderedAccessViewUint()` in the first place.
author: H3x4g0n

reviews.body: Ok, makes sense. The debug layer also seems happy so let's go with it.

commits.message: Use ClearUnorderedAccessViewFloat() instead of ClearUnorderedAccessViewUint() in command_clear_color_texture()

title: [3.x] Use approximate test when comparing properties, thoroughly
body:
I found the need for this when I realized derived scenes of one containing an `AnimationPlayer` were getting the blend times array duplicated on save.

Something like this: `blend_times = [ "cast", "stopped_caster", 0.3, "dodge", "stopped", 0.3, "dodge", "walk", 0.3, [...]`

Due to the `0.3` being processed differently for some reason in different places, the numeric imprecission would end up causing a comparison between something like `0.300001` and `2.999999`.

It may be good to hunt down the exact cause for the difference. However, since we are already applying loose comparison for cases like these, I've just extended the approximate comparison framework to work exhaustively.

It would be great to forward-port this to 4.x, but the codebase differs too much and it would require separate study.

---
This PR is proudly donated by [Pedrocorp](http://pedrocorp.net).
author: RandomShaper

reviews.body:
Looks worth a go to me.

Will leave this a few days before merging, just in case anyone else wants to chime in with reviews / thoughts.
