title: Fix inconsistent SSR blurriness on rough surfaces.
body:
### Description

The existing implementation of SSR has a subtle visual artifact: reflections on rough surfaces have inconsistent look at different distances. Specifically, they become more glossy as the camera moves away.

This PR fixed this issue with minor code changes and performance hit (literally just an additional `+ 1.0` in the shader). The comparison between existing implementation and this PR looks like this:

![ssr_enhancement](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/e93bd202-2459-4015-b9c2-81593fdcdb55)

### Explanation

The problem of existing implementation is that it computes the mip level as `log2(/*screen_space_blur_amout*/)`, but mip level is, strictly speaking, logarithmic to the diameter of sampling region in the texture. When the blur amount is 0, this value should be 1 (because we are sampling from a single pixel). But the computed blur amount is derived from the screen space length of reflected light and a roughness-derived cone radius. This term is 0 when no blur should be applied. Adding 1 to it before taking log2 fixes the math.

### Note

You may have noticed that even with this PR, the look of reflection is still slightly shifted as the distance to camera increases. This is to be expected since we are approximating a real low pass filter with the mipmaps. Mip 0 is always sharp, so mixing it with mip 1 would always produce slight visual artifacts.

EDIT: I just realized that I used Lanczos3 when upscaling the 4.0m and 10.0m images, this makes them sharper than perceived. If you take a closer look at the un-zoomed images (or simply open the test project with a patched engine), you will see that they are fairly consistent with the commit from this PR.

### Test Project

The project used to generate the above comparison is here: [ssr_enhancement_demo.zip](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/25096315/ssr_enhancement_demo.zip)

* *Bugsquad edit, fixes: https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues/115906*
author: HEX-23

reviews.body: Tested locally, it works as expected. Code looks good to me. I've double-checked that SSR roughness is still *mostly* resolution-independent. (It will still eventually decrease in roughness if the internal viewport resolution gets very low though.) ### Scaling = 1.0, FSR2 *Note that FSR2 applies its own mipmap LOD bias, similar to the **Texture Mipmap Bias** project setting. This bias affects screen-space reflections, which is probably a bug (similar to how it affects how reflection probes are sampled).* Before | After -|- | ### Scaling = 0.5, FSR2 *Note that FSR2 applies its own mipmap LOD bias, which is more significant at `0.5` scaling compared to `1.0`.* Before | After -|- |

Makes sense to me. The derivatives for texture sampling with the maximum mipmap never really reach 0 so it makes sense to add the same guarantee here.

You should also add a comment explaining why like Calinou mentioned.

commits.message: Fix inconsistent SSR blurriness on rough surfaces.

title: Fix documentation for `get_velocity_at_local_position`
body:
Resolves godotengine/godot-docs#7110

There is a misleading description of the expected space of `local_position` parameter of `get_velocity_at_local_position` function of both `PhysicsDirectBodyState2D` and `PhysicsDirectBodyState3D` classes.

See the docs issue, but briefly, by passing an vector in local space of the body (both translation and rotation), gives incorrect values, by passing an vector in local translation, but global rotation (meaning an offset product of `global_point` - `body.transform.origin`) it returns the correct velocity value in global space.

The function has the same behaviour of `apply_force` for the parameter, expecting an vector in local translation space, but global rotation space.

It's ideal to rename the function to something like `get_velocity_at_offset`, but this goes outside the scope of this PR and the docs update is more urgent.
author: ghsoares

commits.message: Fixed documentation for get_velocity_at_local_position

title: Fix erroneous use of atomic in the dirty flags of Node3D
body:
Addresses [issue#116011](https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues/116011), and potentially some other issues. The MRP of that issue should now have correct behavior when the commit of this PR is applied.

Behavior of the MRP of [issue#116011](https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues/116011), with this PR:

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/117079db-07c0-4b7c-8353-501c859d4048

## Explanation

Node3D simultaneously stores a `Transform3D` and a pair of euler angles and scale for some reason (documented in `node_3d.h`, right above definition of `enum TransformDirty`). Both representations are readable and writable.

It uses a dirty-flagging system to transparently handle this: when transform is set, euler angle and scale is flagged as dirty; when euler angle or scale is set, transform is flagged as dirty. As an optimization, actual update of the data is deferred until they are read.

In multi-threading mode (i.e., when thread group is set to sub), this dirty flag is implemented as a bitset based on atomic uint32 (`SafeNumeric<uint32_t>`, which is a wrapper over `std::atomic<uint32_t>`).

The problem is, when testing for certain bits in the atomic, what Node3D did is `flags.bit_and(mask)`. However, `SafeNumeric<>::bit_and` is implemented as `std::atomic<>::fetch_and`, whose semantic is "atomically set the atomic variable `v` to `v & mask`, and return the original value of `v`".

This would result in the following problem: any bit set in the dirty flag would cause a query with any mask to return true. And if the query is using a mask that *should* return false, the dirty flag will be effectively cleared.

The correct semantic should be "load the value of `v` and return `v & mask`, without modifying `v`". So it should be implemented as `flags.load() & mask`, instead.

This PR did exactly this.

- Bugsquad: Fixes #116012
author: HEX-23

reviews.body:
Looks good to me. My only concern is that the behaviour is changed a bit since `data.dirty.mt` was previously modified. 

Given that `_test_dirty_bits()` is a const function, and the usage pattern is typically `if(_test_dirty_bits()) { _clear_dirty_bits();}`. I think it is safe to say that this is the correct behaviour

commits.message: Fix erroneous use of atomic in the dirty flags of Node3D

title: Fix `set_process_thread_group()` and transform notification of threaded-processing Node3Ds.
body:
Fixes [issue#115999](https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues/115999). The MRP of that issue should now have correct behavior when the commit of this PR is applied.

Behavior with this PR (in either case):

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/b4c0bf93-785c-4099-8cc8-f28698f251df

## Explanation

Technically, there are two different problems here. But they are highly correlated in actual use cases, so I decided to make a single issue and a single PR. I am going to explain the cause and fix of the two cases described in the issue one by one:

### Case 1, Multiple Node Subtrees using Threaded Processing

When multiple node trees are using threaded processing, visual transform of the meshes randomly get stuck.

`VisualInstance3D`, along with some other `Node3D` based nodes, rely on the transform changed notification to function properly. They update transform of corresponding instances in the rendering server when notified.

Under the hood, they register their own `xform_change` field to a global registry `SceneTree::xform_change_list` when their transform is changed via code, and the `SceneTree` would scan the global registry after all processing is done for a given frame, and issue the notifications accordingly.

The problem is, `SceneTree::xform_change_list` is not thread-safe to modify. The original code checks `is_accessible_from_caller_thread()` to decide whether the operation of adding `xform_change` to `SceneTree::xform_change_list` should be deferred or not.

This would only trigger when we e.g. change the transform of a node in main thread, and it has a child in another thread. When multiple threads are running in parallel, they are free to attempt to modify `SceneTree::xform_change_list` concurrently because `is_accessible_from_caller_thread()` is true for each of them. This leads to a race condition, resulting in `SceneTree::xform_change_list`, which is a linked list, being dangerously malformed.

By checking `Thread::is_main_thread()` instead, this PR defers every attempt to modify `SceneTree::xform_change_list` from threaded nodes to the main thread, resolving the race condition.

### Case 2, Setting `process_thread_group` in Code

Setting `process_thread_group` in the code at runtime does not yield the expected behavior. Namely, the child nodes are not properly setup to reside in the same thread group.

The origin of this problem seems like a typo in the codebase, `Node::_add_tree_to_process_thread_group()` is not recursively modifying the child nodes as expected (instead of calling `_add_tree_to_process_thread_group` of its children, it called `_add_to_process_thread_group`).

This PR changed it and rearranged its code so that some internal info is properly setup before registering to the thread group.
author: HEX-23

reviews.body:
Makes sense!

title: Fix transform updates sometimes being discarded when using Jolt
body:
Fixes #115322.

This fixes an issue when using Jolt Physics, where if you performed a physics query during the body state synchronization (e.g. `_integrate_forces`) you could end up effectively discarding pending transform updates of newly added bodies.

This fixes that by limiting `JoltBodyActivationListener3D` to only be set during the simulation step.
author: mihe

commits.message:
Fix transform updates sometimes being discarded when using Jolt

Co-authored-by: Jorrit Rouwe <jrouwe@gmail.com>

So this fixes a regression 4.5+.

title: Network: Normalize IP parsing, fix IPv6, tests
body:
This PR normalize IP parsing across `String` and `IPAddress`, fixes many cases of incorrect parsing, and adds many test cases to validate the new parser.

The parser is designed to do zero heap allocations, although I'm not particularly fond of the code (parsing IPv6 is hard :sob: ), the result is much better than before and makes `String.is_valid_ip_address` consistent with `IPAddress.is_valid`.

Closes #114245 (superseded)
Closes #112554 (superseded)
Closes #99624 (superseded)
Fixes #112511

title: Fix TextEdit IME error on mouse over
body: - Fixes hovering over a line with IME text causing errors `ERROR: scene/gui/text_edit.cpp:6402 - Condition "p_column > text[p_line].length()" is true. Returning: 0` - Fixes search highlight being offset when there is IME text before it - Fixes minimap not being correct when using IME (wrapped lines were messed up) - Fixes (maybe) https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues/110316 - I'm not sure if this is the same issue or not - Supersedes https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/110787 since that fixes the mouse over issue - But I don't think it actually fixes () https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues/110138 since that describes Chinese IME specific issues and does not mention mouse movement. Fixed `get_line_wrap_index_at_column` not accepting column with IME. `get_line_wrapped_text` used `text.get_line_wrap_ranges` which uses `data_buf` for the line wraps, which is uses IME text. So it was using the non-IME text string with IME line wraps, which was causing the minimap and search highlight issues. Also made `get_line_wrap_index_at_column` use the `get_line_wrap_ranges` directly, it doesn't need to get the text first. There are lots more issues with IME here, anything using the TextServer or `data_buf` is using the text with the IME, but some functions use those together with things that don't use the IME text, like the carets, selections, and text strings. Such as `get_rect_at_line_column`, `get_word`, most places that use `get_line_column_at_pos`, and more. Search highlight issue (with an open find panel):
author: kitbdev

commits.message: Fix TextEdit IME error on mouse over

title: Select relevant 3D lights per mesh on GLES3 and Mobile renderers
body:
This PR changes the behavior of the `RendererSceneCull::_scene_cull` function to select the most relevant 3D lights per mesh in the Compatibility and Mobile renderers.  It is a fix for [Issue 107070](https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues/107070).

Previously, if the scene had many more lights in range than could be rendered per-mesh (so > 8 for Mobile, or > `Max Lights per Object` for Compatibility), Godot would simply pick the first N lights and assign them to the mesh.  Even increasing the limits (on Compatibility) failed to illuminate the scene as you would expect.  I am sure most users who need complex 3D lighting can use the Forward+ renderer, but I am hoping to get a decent approximation running on the more limited renderers (I ran into the issue trying to fake GI in a procedural building with lots of lights).

Here is the behavior of 4.4.1 in a scene with 256 meshes and 256 omni-lights.  For testing purposes, the range of each light was extended so that all meshes were in range of all lights.  The result looked the same on both Mobile and Compatibility (with limits set to 256 lights per scene, 8 per-mesh to match Mobile):
![image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/84221bad-476d-4bcf-9e86-fe5cf483c16c)

With the code modifications in this PR, it now looks like this:
![image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/56710ad3-3227-456e-8334-1da10a872958)

The original code ran through each mesh, and then each light relative to each mesh.  The new code follows the same pattern but now assigns a score to each light, relative to each mesh, factoring in the distance from the light's AABB center to the mesh's AABB center, the light range, and the light energy.

The code then keeps track of the top N lights, by keeping the top N scores in a SortArray, which uses a heap internally to keep the scores sorted enough to find the worst score directly (it is always in slot [0]).  If a new light is found to score better than the worst in the collection, slot 0 is replaced with the new light and the heap is updated.  

In theory this is the fastest way to run through *all* M lights while keeping the top N, however many spatial hashing schemes could accelerate this by not having to run through all M lights for every mesh.  So there is room to optimize further if this turns out to be too slow for extreme cases, however 256 3D lights in a scene might be excessive for mobile and web targets.  [8^)

- _Bugsquad edit,_ fixes https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues/107070
author: jon1solution

reviews.body:
Tested locally with the MRP from https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues/107070, it works as expected. (It doesn't have as many meshes/lights as shown on the screenshots, but it still shows that the situation compared to `master` is improved by rendering small lights on top of all the meshes.)

Code looks good to me.

Looks good with the latest feedback.

It solves the issue as described at the top, choosing the closest lights to the mesh being lit.

It doesn't solve the issue referenced later but also isn't intended to fix that. I don't think that issue can be fixed in a meaningful way and should simply be worked around by breaking a large mesh up into smaller chunks, in which case this PR does its job really nicely.

commits.message:
Keeping the top N lights per mesh in the culling phase.

Previously, each mesh was paired up with the first N of each Omni- and Spot-lights.
Now each light is scored based on its distance to the mesh, energy, and range.
The new code uses a heap to track the best N lights (of each type) for predictability.

title: Make directional lights' tighter shadow caster culling truly tight (implement per-cascade culling)
body: This PR fixes errors in the implementation of tight shadowcaster culling for directional lights, and implements tight per-cascade culling for directional lights. I have not performed strict performance benchmarks, but have observed improved GPU times in my own project, and I believe that benchmarking aside, the shadowmaps speak for themselves: best-case scenario 4.5-stable: best-case scenario PR: worst-case scenario 4.5-stable: worst-case scenario PR: Tests were done with this MRP: [shadow_cull_mrp.zip](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/24472116/shadow_cull_mrp.zip) The plane in the MRP is intentionally made to only receive and not cast shadows so that the cubes are more easily visible in the shadowmaps. I have already tested 4, 2, and 1 split directional shadows and they should all work correctly. There should be no visual changes introduced by this PR, as this PR should only improve performance while maintaining the same visuals. So far I've fixed any visual issues I've found and everything should work fine, but more subtle edge cases might've slipped by without me noticing, so some extra tests that everything is working fine are welcome. Do your worst :P Worth noting that despite what the shadowmaps of 4.5-stable seem to imply, there was actually no previous implementation of tight per-cascade culling. Previous directional tight culling only culled around the camera's view frustum and *not* the actual cascades' visibility ranges; then another separate per-cascade AABB culling is performed, which is separate from tight culling, and is why it *looks* like there is tight per-cascade culling, even though there actually wasn't. Note to reviewers: [I've pointed this out in the code](https://github.com/Image-unavailable/godot/blob/95ab1726e928a4561906fc2c79dfdab3786c8a90/servers/rendering/rendering_light_culler.h#L207), but I'm not sure whether the `_is_colinear_tri` function actually does anything useful. It might be worth removing (minimal performance gains?), but I don't know if it's there for some particular reason or whatnot, since lawnjelly must've added it for some reason (even though it backfired due to too-high thresholds causing many false-positives). Then again I did have occasional engine freezes while working on this PR with it disabled among other changes (idk if removing `_is_colinear_tri` caused them though), so maybe it's better to leave it there... *This PR's name is a reference to lawnjelly's original "tighter shadow caster culling" #84745 PR.*
author: Image-unavailable

reviews.body: Approve based on content, although I suspect this could use another pass by @AThousandShips our resident formatting guru, as I'm not sure it meets all our formatting requirements, things like having a space between `//` and comments.
Style looks good
Looks great! Amazing work!

commits.message: Make directional lights' tighter shadowcaster culling truly tight (implement per-cascade culling)

title: Fix CI errors after recent merge batches
body:
When syncing my fork today for a rebase, fork CI reported the following error:
https://github.com/StarryWorm/godot/actions/runs/23911165773/job/69733515404

This PR fixes this by removing those unused includes.

I believe these includes were made unused by the changes in #117774, but were not flagged by CI at the time because those files were not in the diff files.
For `script_language.cpp`, that file was modified by #115157, whose checks were run before my PR (and before clangd-tidy-diff was added to CI). When I synced my fork, the now-unused include got picked up by clangd-tidy-diff. 
For `test_main.cpp`, same story but with #116798 

These kinds of second-hand changes caused by include PRs indirectly causing unused includes in unmodified files may happen again in the future. 
Note to self and all others working on #111218, when we build these PRs, we might want to run clangd-tidy on the entire codebase.

Edit:
Also fixes an XML error caused by recent doc style changes, see comments below
author: StarryWorm

reviews.body: I would prefer the commits squashed, this is just fixing CI check errors due to merge/rebase timing collisions.

commits.message: Fix CI errors

title: Add a nearest-neighbor scaling option to Viewport's Scaling 3D Mode property
body:
This is useful for 3D games with a pixel art appearance, or when using a resolution scale of `0.5` to improve performance without compromising crispness too much when not using FSR 1.0.

The property hints now allow decreasing the scale further to accomodate for pixel art use cases, as well as increased precision in the value (useful for a scale of `0.3333`).

This is supported in all renderers.

I've made sure that effects relying on bilinear filtering to look correct (such as glow and depth of field) still work correctly. No Vulkan validation errors are reported when running with `--gpu-validation` on either rendering method.

**Testing project:** [test_scaling_3d_nearest.zip](https://github.com/godotengine/godot/files/12719856/test_scaling_3d_nearest.zip)

- This closes https://github.com/godotengine/godot-proposals/issues/4697.

## Preview

![Screenshot_20230721_014711](https://github.com/godotengine/godot/assets/180032/f2757542-05bf-4697-a574-a5bb0f5dc03f)
author: Calinou

reviews.body: Tested, working as expected.

Looks good now! Thanks for taking another look

Should be good once you figure out the compatibility issue. It might early out if any the scale_3d is anything but zero in the viewport renderer code
I think [this comment](https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/79731/files#r2565719927) needs some looking into and a decision before this is merged.

Thanks for the updates. This almost looks good to me.

In addition to my [previous code formatting comment](https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/79731/changes#r2565222837), ~~I noticed that the debanding logic has not been updated, so I've added another change request comment with the fix for that (including a comment that describes how this code should work).~~

Edit: my mistake. The current debanding logic in master isn't entirely correct. Once it's corrected by https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/114890, I don't think there will be any need to touch these lines.

I'm a bit curious why `0.1` is the minimum scale... I tried setting it lower, but it still seems to be forced to a minimum of `0.1`. Not a big deal at all, just something I don't understand. My guess is that if you want to have a pixelated screen transition where the nearest neighbour scaling is animated, you might want to go quite a bit lower than 0.1. (Edit: actually, animating this scale is probably a horrible idea because it would be quite slow?) Do you happen to know why this can't be done with the current PR? Not a blocker, mind you!

If I'm reading this code correctly, an additional pass is always required when using Nearest, which means that it is always slower than Bilinear. Because of this, it should be documented as such.

...But does this really need to be the case?? Is there any way that, when SMAA and spatial upscaler are not used, that Nearest could be implemented without this additional pass?

If so, better to improve this PR so that Nearest is actually as fast as bilinear and ignore these suggested changes to the documentation.

Now that so many of the underlying bugs have been fixed, the code of this PR looks really clean and simple. Looking great!

I've added a couple of comments for things that need to be addressed. In the spirit of moving quickly, I'm posting the comments now and will continue to test after posting this message to see if I find any issues.
All of the issues that I've been able to find with behaviour and code have been addressed. This looks clean, simple, and bug-free -- Ready to merge!
Looks and works great!

commits.message:
Add a nearest-neighbor scaling option to Viewport's Scaling 3D Mode property

This is useful for 3D games with a pixel art appearance, or when
using a resolution scale of `0.5` to improve performance without
compromising crispness too much when not using FSR 1.0.

The property hints now allow decreasing the scale further to accomodate
for pixel art use cases, as well as increased precision in the value
(useful for a scale of `0.3333`).

Co-authored-by: Daniel Savage <dansvg@gmail.com>
Co-authored-by: Kaleb Reid <78945904+Kaleb-Reid@users.noreply.github.com>

title: Refactor `blas_create` to accept multiple geometries.
body:
Refactors `blas_create` to accept multiple geometries. What I have in mind with this is having each `AccelerationStructureGeometry` represent a `Mesh::Surface`, and create a BLAS from `Mesh`/`MeshInstance` objects. 

With the previous approach, each `Mesh::Surface` would have to be its own BLAS. That would not be very efficient due to AABB overlaps and increased TLAS instance count.

Further changes to note:
- Taking the vertex/index buffers instead of the vertex/index arrays. I felt the old approach was a little strange since the BLAS does not care about the other components in the vertex array at all. I think it is cleaner to expect the buffers directly.
- Making the BLAS dependent on the buffers and properly bookkeeping their draw trackers. 

To do:
- [x] Documentation for the new class.

Example: https://github.com/blueskythlikesclouds/godot-raytracing-gdscript-demo/commit/2d575518edd4596c9724a1e72e11ee6115673cde
author: blueskythlikesclouds

reviews.body:
Ok, read through the changes before bed and so far what I have seen make sense.

Mind you, I have never really tried to do raytracing stuff myself, so there might be some considerations I am missing due to my lack of practical experience, but at least in theory this looks good to me.

Documentations looks good as well, but maybe you could upload your rt gdscript test project somewhere people will actually find it?

While I am certain that any experienced rendering engineer will have no trouble figuring out how to properly configure the ASGeometry (should be trivial with a look at the vulkan rt docs and maybe some knowledge about how godot expose the flags and format enums) I think it would still be nice to have some example code available for users to reference.


@fire I don't think any performance benchmarks will be necessary for this PR. The RT API in godot is still experimental and this is a clear improvement that is in line with the current industry standards. We really should get this in now before people start using the RT API in it's current form.

commits.message: Refactor blas_create to accept multiple geometries.

title: Android: Allow implementing java interfaces from GDScript
body:
Fixes https://github.com/godotengine/godot-proposals/issues/13905

This PR removes one of the limitations of `JavaClassWrapper` by allowing to create proxy instances that implements Java interfaces, thus allowing users to create and pass arbitrary callbacks other than `Runnable` and `java.util.concurrent.Callable`.

This is done by leveraging the [`java.lang.reflect.Proxy` class](https://developer.android.com/reference/java/lang/reflect/Proxy) which relies on an associated [`InvocationHandler`](https://developer.android.com/reference/java/lang/reflect/InvocationHandler) to handle invocations on the methods of the implemented interfaces. In Godot, that `InvocationHandler` itself delegates its implementation to an user-passed Godot `Callable`, or `Object`.

Example with `Callable`:
```
# The callable must be able to handle the arguments expected by the SAM interface method, and provide the expected return type.
var cb = func (content: String) -> void:
	print(content)

# The callback is created by passing the callable, and the SAM interface to implement.
var callback = JavaClassWrapper.create_sam_callback("android.util.Printer", cb)

# Invoking the 'println(...)' method from the 'android.util.Printer' interface.
callback.println("Hello Godot World!")
```

Example with `Object`:
```
# The object's methods signatures must match the methods signatures of the interfaces to implement.
class PrintProxy:
	func println(content: String) -> void:
		print(content)

var print_proxy = PrintProxy.new()

# The proxy object is created by passing the object instance and the list of interfaces to implement.
var printer_object = JavaClassWrapper.create_proxy(print_proxy, ["android.util.Printer"])

# Invoking the 'println(...)' method from the 'android.util.Printer' interface.
printer_object.println("Hello Godot World!")
```

**Note:**
A proxy instance can only implement java interfaces, so there's still a limitation with creating instances from abstract classes.

<!--
Please target the `master` branch in priority.

Relevant fixes are cherry-picked for stable branches as needed by maintainers.

To speed up the contribution process and avoid CI errors, please set up pre-commit hooks locally:
https://contributing.godotengine.org/en/latest/engine/guidelines/code_style.html
-->

title: Fix decal atlas clipped when using texture resolution < 32
body:
This fixes issue #108033.

When a texture's resolution in a decal atlas was < 32, the border margin was no longer taken into account. Resulting in cropped textures in the atlas.

The fix might sound unintuitive, but basically an `atlas_height` of 1 = border size (32).
So, an `atlas_height` of 2 = border size * 2. Which is the minimum size required to insert textures into the atlas.

- *Bugsquad:* fixes #108033
author: Gaktan

reviews.body: Tested locally (rebased on top of `master` babc272d44ecfbd86e3b3fc5397ef936b1ce12d8), it works as expected. Code looks good to me. I've tested various texture sizes in PointLight2D and Decal, from 1×1/2×2 to large uneven sizes, and it renders correctly. Before | After -|- |

commits.message: Fix decal atlas clipped when using texture resolution < 32

title: [.Net] Implement `AddRange(ROS)` for `Godot.Collections.Array(<T>)`
body:
As the Godot Arrays (script-language-wise) are effectively C# Lists, this PR implements `AddRange(ReadOnlySpan)` overloads to the corresponding C# wrapper type to match the same implementation in [CollectionExtensions.cs](https://github.com/dotnet/runtime/blob/87b35f477c09f146107a6c11627317f6b15e9d3e/src/libraries/System.Private.CoreLib/src/System/Collections/Generic/CollectionExtensions.cs#L96).

This also benefits existing codes, as all AddRange calls with collection expressions and arrays automatically bind to the new ROS overload (after recompilation). This removes GC-tracked objects (for collection expressions) or fewer virtual calls (for arrays).
author: Delsin-Yu

reviews.body: Looks good to me. We can remove the `T[]` overloads if we manage to upgrade to .NET 10 before the stable release.

title: Rework copy-pasting section/category values
body:
Another follow-up to #111469 (is there anything left from the original code? 🤔)

Copying section/category values had a major flaw cause by how the properties were copied. The inspector relied on Object's `get_property_list()`, which could return list that does not exactly match what is displayed in the inspector (due to various special cases). The code to extract properties belonging to sections/categories was a bit hacky and reliable.

This PR changes it to happen completely on the inspector side. The properties will now register themselves in the parent section/category, so when copying values, they are copied from the list of EditorProperties, instead of object's properties. They are pasted into the EditorProperties too. This allowed to simplify the code (no more special cases for MultiNodeEdit etc.).

- Fixes #116889
- Fixes the above bug for shader parameters (idk if there is open issue)
- Fixes being able to paste section values into read-only properties
- Fixes errors and resetting values when property name is not in clipboard (regression from #117600)
author: KoBeWi

reviews.body: Fixes the issues, and the code looks fine.

commits.message: Rework copy-pasting section/category values

When an editor uses warped panning and is floating, the panning is completely broken.

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/4c8a4e07-52b7-4a20-9e74-bddaeb28991c

This PR changes ViewPanner to setup with a child of the viewport instead and get the viewport at the time of panning.

title: Fix crash in `GDScript::prepare_compilation` error message
body:
## Description
Issue: #117699
_Maintainer edit: Fixes #117699_

## Technical details
In prepare_compilation condition checks base.is_null() and then calls its properties.
```
if (base.is_null()) {
     _set_error(vformat(R"(Could not find class "%s" in "%s".)", base->fully_qualified_name, base->path), nullptr);
     return ERR_COMPILATION_FAILED;
}
```

The error caused by 'uid://' path handling in GDScript *GDScript::find_class(const String &p_qualified_name), because its function does not support such path properly.
And that creates a question: should gdscript allow inheritance from "uid://[...]" paths?

The PR primary focuses to fix a crash.
author: M4rchy-S

reviews.body:
I can confirm that this fixes the crash. 

However replacing `base->fully_qualified_name` with `base_root->fully_qualified_name` in this context is not correct. In the case of inner classes, `base_root` is the outer script, while `base` would be the inner. You should save the fully qualified name before the `find_class` call and use it in the error message.

(I will say that this is a pretty theoretical remark, since I can't actually get the issue to trigger. Inner classes will already fail in `_gdtype_from_datatype` for the setup from the issue so they never reach the code touched here.)

Can confirm that this fixes the crash.

Code seems correct to me, that being said the changes might do touch more lines than necessary. However it's kinda is consistent within this block of code I guess.

commits.message: Fix crash in compilation error message

title: Fix CanvasItem lines appearing thicker when antialiasing is enabled
body:
This was due to the antialiasing feather not being compensated for when drawing the line. The feather makes the line appear thicker, so we have to reduce the line width to compensate.

This also affected filled circles and rectangles, which are now compensated for by adjusting their radius and size respectively.

- This closes https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues/105027.

## Preview

*Click to view at full size. All screenshots taken from the MRP of https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues/105027 with the Compatibility rendering method.*

### Lines

*Note that stretched ellipses/arcs using `draw_set_transform()` are not compensated for by the current antialiasing code (this is possible but nontrivial). The MRP uses those instead of https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/85080, which make use of the antialiasing fix implemented in this PR.*

Not Antialiased | Antialiased Before | Antialiased After
-|-|-
![Screenshot_20250407_181643](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/e9aa79f8-8bcb-412f-b35e-5b38534fb4fd) | ![Screenshot_20250407_181656](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/eaf5ae7e-36da-421c-9545-287f11e650d8) | ![Screenshot_20250407_181710](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/ba757eb5-7b68-4516-97da-f0cafc1d23f1)

### Rectangles

*Note that StyleBoxFlat antialiasing is not affected by this issue, so this PR doesn't change it any way.*

Not Antialiased | Antialiased Before | Antialiased After
-|-|-
![Screenshot_20250407_181644](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/f22b5f39-b67d-4e49-8033-f86cf1c15ed9) | ![Screenshot_20250407_181657](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/9f3f8cf5-033a-41fb-846b-eaae553461b5) | ![Screenshot_20250407_181712](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/3b7877f0-3ea8-4a2f-9053-3162bb1f4667)

### Polygons

Not Antialiased | Antialiased Before | Antialiased After
-|-|-
![Screenshot_20250407_181646](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/f0854dfc-d5e8-4138-909f-da8520392997) | ![Screenshot_20250407_181659](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/6b7baf09-88f4-4962-bf1b-6684978c6a0d) | ![Screenshot_20250407_181714](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/0a2be92b-213c-4918-9a91-152b4894c83c)
author: Calinou

Fix CanvasItem lines appearing thicker when antialiasing is enabled

This was due to the antialiasing feather not being compensated for

so we have to reduce the line width to compensate.

This also affected filled circles and rectangles, which are now
compensated for by adjusting their radius and size respectively.

title: Add support for saving HDR screenshots to `Image.save_exr` functions.
body: ### What problem(s) does this PR solve? - Fixes #117795 ### What is the rationale for the approach used in this PR? `Image.save_exr` functions have been modified to add two new parameters: 1. `color_image: bool` - Set `color_image` to `true` when saving a color image, such as a screenshot. Negative values will be included when `color_image` is `false`, which may be useful for saving raw floating point data such as a lightmap that includes negative light information. - When `true`, negative values are clipped to `0.0` to give correct colour rendering for screenshots. 3. `max_linear_value` - Color component values in the resulting EXR file will not exceed `max_linear_value` if `max_linear_value` is not negative. - When saving screenshots of a project that uses HDR output, use `Window.get_output_max_linear_value` for `max_linear_value`. **Clipping negative values (`color_image == true`)** Internal to Godot, negative color values represent negative radiant energy for the purposes of lighting simulation. But for the purposes of CIE colorimetry, which is used for HDR image formats such as EXR, negative RGB values represent colors that are outside of the current RGB gamut and are positive RGB values in other color gamuts. For this reason, negative values should never be saved to EXR files that are intended to represent a color image such as a screenshot. This PR adds parameters to the existing `save_exr` and `save_exr_to_buffer` functions to allow saving of HDR screenshots. **New `max_linear_value` parameter** The new `max_linear_value` parameter matches [`Window.get_output_max_linear_value()`](https://docs.godotengine.org/en/latest/classes/class_window.html#class-window-method-get-output-max-linear-value). This means that producing an EXR image that is identical to the rendering presented by Godot is as simple as: ``` C++ get_window().get_texture().get_image().save_exr("screenshot.exr", false, get_window().get_output_max_linear_value()) ``` With these changes, it is now possible to save an image that is identical to the image presented by HDR output: In the above renderings: the red, green, and blue rectangles shown on the bottom left corner contain negative values, such as those that may be created by negative lights. The colour sweep that comprises the majority of the render exceeds values of `1.0` on the right side. ### Was generative AI (LLM AI) used to create a portion of this PR? No. ### Are there any parts of this PR that you are uncertain of or require special attention from reviewers? - I haven't yet tested `SRC_FLOAT`. I don't know how to do this, but if it's possible to test this code path it should be done before merging. Anyone know how to?
author: allenwp

reviews.body:
I have not done a technical review, but the amendment of allowing backwards compatibily with exr saving makes sense to me.

The default being the same behaviour as the current stable is correct, but I haven't checked it.
Code looks good to me

commits.message: Add support for saving HDR screenshots to `Image.save_exr` functions.

title: Extra particle tools for seeking
body:
There are some instances when playing particles "artificially" (seeking) where particles need to process, but also need to not emit. For example, if there's a oneshot particle that emits for 0.2s and the lifetime is 1s, we will have a total of 1.2s of play time.
But there was currently no way to do that 1s lifetime, in a single frame, without emitting for the whole 1.2s.

This PR adds the ability to actually seek the particle system to both a state when it's emitting, and a state where it's still processing but not emitting, in a single frame.

body:
<!--
Please target the `master` branch in priority.

Relevant fixes are cherry-picked for stable branches as needed by maintainers.

To speed up the contribution process and avoid CI errors, please set up pre-commit hooks locally:

https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/113133 added the debugger to the new dock system but disabled the ability to float due to the debugger being set to nullptr on exiting the tree, which happens during a reparent (which happens when making the dock float).

title: Rework TileSet editor proxy objects
body:
Fixes #117406

This changes proxy objects used in TileSet editor from singular Object to RefCounted. Every time something is edited, a new proxy object is created specifically for this item, so undo actions are created properly for this specific item. The lifetime of proxy objects is managed by UndoRedo, so this shouldn't cause memory problems.

title: Enable clang-tidy's performance-move-const-arg check
body:
This PR enables the clang-tidy check [`performance-move-const-arg`](https://clang.llvm.org/extra/clang-tidy/checks/performance/move-const-arg.html). This check validates that when `<utility>`'s [`std::move`](https://en.cppreference.com/w/cpp/utility/move.html) is used, that the result of invoking `move` matches expected behavior (i.e. actually doing a move and not silently falling back to a copy).

There is not a compiler-level warning for this issue on Linux (I don't see a comparable warning for GCC/Clang). MSVC has [C26478](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/cpp/code-quality/c26478?view=msvc-170) which covers the same issue there.
author: Shadows-of-Fire

reviews.body:
The change and check make sense. 

The `GDType` change isn't a bug, but I was using `std::move` unnecessarily (the callee uses const refs instead of identities for lookup).

Only caveat i have is that this check may not be needed, since barely anyone uses `std::move`. Do you happen to know how much time adding this check adds to overall check time?

commits.message: Enable clang-tidy's performance-move-const-arg check

title: OpenXR: Add eye-tracked foveation setting, and enable subsampled images by default
body:
In theory, enabling eye-tracked foveated rendering (when it's available on the hardware) should always be a win, which is why we didn't originally add a setting for it.

However, there is currently a bug in Pico's OpenXR runtime, where enabling it will cause a crash. It's Pico's bug and they'll fix it eventually, but in order to allow our users to work around it, this PR adds a setting for it which is enabled by default. This can allow developers to use feature tags to disable it on specific platforms, and then remove the workaround later after Pico fixes the underlying bug.

Similarly, foveation using Vulkan subsampled images should almost always be a win too, but we already added a setting for it, just in case. :-) This PR changes that setting to also be enabled by default
author: dsnopek

commits.message: OpenXR: Add eye-tracked foveation setting, and enable subsampled images by default

title: Fix breakpoints resetting on external editor save
body:
Currently, Godot-defined breakpoints disappear when a file is saved from an external editor. This fixes it by restoring the previous editor state upon reloading a file. Therefore, it also applies to bookmarks and folded sections, as they had the same issue and it was easier to fix all of them at once this way.

This new behavior has better consistency, since previously the breakpoints were cleared, but only if the file was opened in Godot's script editor. Now, breakpoints are never cleared, which is also more similar to what external editors such as vscode do when they receive external changes.

It also fixes a related error, which was triggered if you:
1. Had a file with breakpoint at line n
2. Closed its editor in Godot
3. Edited the file externally to have m < n lines
4. Reopened the editor. You would expect this error:
`Index p_line = (n-1) is out of bounds (get_line_count() = m)`

The fix is included in this PR as it was necessary for the other changes and is harmless.

Fixes #116672.
author: p-b-b

reviews.body:
Tested and it fixes the issue.
Makes sense.

There are some preexisting issues with the get/set edit state that are more easily visible now, like how the scroll_position doesn't work right if there are folded lines above it (the top or center line number should be stored instead of the v scroll) or folded lines can fold the wrong line if the line it was on was moved externally (we could have some nearby text saved with it or something).
These should be fixed later though.

commits.message: Fix breakpoints resetting on external editor save

title: Add vector components to 3D ruler tool
body:
<!--
Please target the `master` branch in priority.

Relevant fixes are cherry-picked for stable branches as needed by maintainers.

To speed up the contribution process and avoid CI errors, please set up pre-commit hooks locally:
https://docs.godotengine.org/en/latest/contributing/development/code_style_guidelines.html

https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/100162 added ruler functionality to 3D, and this PR builds on-top of it.

Related: https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/100415

The PR enables the 3D ruler tool to display the components of the measurement vector to allow users to get more details similar to the 2D measurement tool. This also fixes some z-fighting issues in the current state of the measurement tool. 

The `shift` key will activate it, since it can be too noisy in specific situations. 

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/13c9f16a-c182-4a9f-b004-d47163c33452
author: ryevdokimov

reviews.body:
I have not done extreme technical review of this, but it's an improvement from a usability point of view for the 3d ruler.

My attempt at doing this was via the concept of measuring tape along on a surface.

https://github.com/V-Sekai/TOOL_geodesiac_path

![image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/3aacd41e-27cf-4539-a883-0ece3cc33482)
Alright, did a bit of going over this code myself. Feel free to go over this review.

Tested locally, it works as expected. Code looks good to me. Some feedback: - I suggest using a different color for the X/Y/Z labels, so they can be distinguished more easily from the main diagonal distance. Right now, the labels can appear very close to each other (if not overlap), which reduces readability. - Ideally, we could reuse the X/Y/Z axis colors from the 3D editor (`axis_x_color` and so on), or an orange color for all axes if that's not feasible. - If it's not too much work, this could also be applied to the lines drawn in the 3D viewport.

commits.message:
Fix theme item inspector tooltips for Window subclasses.

(cherry picked from commit 432214a29edbed233dd80e635806467c1795fbe3)

Revert "[.NET] Remove EFS update on reloading assemblies" but with deferred call

It turns out this call was needed after all, it ensures new scripts are included
in the global class list by updating EFS for them. In my previous PR I must've
only tested scripts that have been opened or loaded by the editor at some point
so I didn't encounter the bug.

This reverts the previous PR but with a deferred call to avoid reintroducing
the bug that the PR fixed. Updating EFS here is still too early, so we defer
the call to ensure the type info is available.

stop autocomplete from eating words by default

(cherry picked from commit e6d5e532e99c9e3765a102cd5a85330da39b78b3)

Wayland: Improve mapping robustness and synchronization

We assumed that a window will be ready after a single roundtrip but
that's actually not guaranteed by the XDG shell protocol.

This patch waits for an arbitrary timeout (10s) before erroring out and
forcefully closing the window. Since this exercised quite some code
paths, it also reworks some window handling logic and fixes a bunch of
synchronization issues.

title: Fix AnimationTree AnimationPlayerEditor broken
body:
- Fixes https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues/117941

I assumed that this code that used to prevent the AnimationPlayerEditor from showing over the AnimationTreeEditor was fine, but it seems that something has changed since it was last used and now there are issues.

https://github.com/godotengine/godot/blob/220b0b2f74d8e089481b140c42a42992a76dd6fc/editor/animation/animation_player_editor_plugin.cpp#L2490-L2491

The logic in `AnimationPlayerEditorPlugin::make_visible` needs to run even if opening the AnimationTreeEditor over it, so I removed the check. 
This also makes the AnimationPlayerEditor show if it is not in the same dock as the AnimationTreeEditor, which is now possible with some of the bottom dock reworks.
But then there needs to be a different way to make sure that the AnimationTreeEditor opens over it. (https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues/115947)
So I changed the order in `EditorData::get_handling_sub_editors`.

The order of sub editor plugins (non main screen ones) was changed in https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/37022 as an addition to the main editor plugins, in order to give user plugins priority. But all sub editor plugins are edited, so to give users priority they should actually be edited after the default ones so they can show over them.
This change also makes plugins edit in the order they are added, the AnimationTreeEditor is added after the AnimationPlayerEditor, so it now shows over it. 
Generally, I think more general plugins are added before more specific ones, so it should make sense to edit the more specific ones after.

I don't think any other sub editors edit the same object and care about order where this would be an issue.
Here are all plugins in the order that they are added to EditorData's `editor_plugins` in a default build. I'm still looking through it to see if there would be any issues from changing it to be in reverse order to forward order for non-main screens, but I haven't found any issues.

<details><summary>All EditorPlugins in order</summary>

1. DebuggerEditorPlugin does not handle any
2. CanvasItemEditorPlugin handles (main screen) `CanvasItem`
3. Node3DEditorPlugin handles (main screen) `Node3D`
4. ScriptEditorPlugin handles (main screen) `Script` || `JSON` || `TextFile`
5. GameViewPlugin (main screen) does not handle any
6. AssetLibraryEditorPlugin (main screen) does not handle any
7. AnimationPlayerEditorPlugin handles `AnimationPlayer` || `AnimationTree` || `AnimationMixer`
8. AnimationTrackKeyEditEditorPlugin handles `AnimationTrackKeyEdit`
9. AnimationMarkerKeyEditEditorPlugin handles `AnimationMarkerKeyEdit`
10. VersionControlEditorPlugin does not handle any
11. AudioBusesEditorPlugin handles `AudioBusLayout`
12. AnimationTreeEditorPlugin handles `AnimationTree`
13. AudioStreamEditorPlugin does not handle any
14. AudioStreamRandomizerEditorPlugin handles none
15. BitMapEditorPlugin does not handle any
16. BoneMapEditorPlugin does not handle any
17. Camera3DEditorPlugin handles `Camera3D`
18. ControlEditorPlugin handles `Control`
19. CPUParticles3DEditorPlugin handles `CPUParticles3D` (ParticlesEditorPlugin)
20. CurveEditorPlugin does not handle any
21. DebugAdapterServer does not handle any
22. EditorScriptPlugin does not handle any
23. FontEditorPlugin does not handle any
24. GPUParticles3DEditorPlugin handles `GPUParticles3D` (ParticlesEditorPlugin)
25. GPUParticlesCollisionSDF3DEditorPlugin handles `GPUParticlesCollisionSDF3D`
26. GradientEditorPlugin does not handle any
27. GradientTexture2DEditorPlugin does not handle any
28. InputEventEditorPlugin does not handle any
29. LightmapGIEditorPlugin handles `LightmapGI`
30. MarginContainerEditorPlugin handles `MarginContainer`
31. MaterialEditorPlugin does not handle any
32. MultiMeshEditorPlugin handles `MultiMeshInstance3D`
33. MeshInstance3DEditorPlugin handles `MeshInstance3D` || `MultiNodeEdit` with 2+ `MeshInstance3D`
34. MeshLibraryEditorPlugin handles `MeshLibrary`
35. MultiMeshEditorPlugin handles `MultiMeshInstance3D`
36. OccluderInstance3DEditorPlugin handles `OccluderInstance3D`
37. PackedSceneEditorPlugin does not handle any
38. Path3DEditorPlugin handles `Path3D`
39. PhysicalBone3DEditorPlugin does not handle any
40. Polygon3DEditorPlugin handles `Node3D` && bool(p_object->call("_is_editable_3d_polygon"))
41. ResourcePreloaderEditorPlugin handles `ResourcePreloader`
42. ShaderEditorPlugin handles `Shader` || `ShaderInclude`
43. ShaderFileEditorPlugin handles `RDShaderFile`

title: Mark template function in math_funcs_binary constexpr
body:
In fixing a compile error of my gdextension I noticed the inconsistency of using `inline`, `inline static`, `constexpr` between `godot-cpp`([`include/godot_cpp/core/defs.hpp`](https://github.com/godotengine/godot-cpp/blob/master/include/godot_cpp/core/defs.hpp))  and `godot` ([`core/math/math_funcs_binary.h`](https://github.com/godotengine/godot/blob/master/core/math/math_funcs_binary.h)). The detail of my issue can be found below:
https://github.com/godotengine/godot-cpp/pull/1962

`godot` uses `constexpr` for most of functions in that file except for the two template functions `is_power_of_2` and `nearest_power_of_2_templated` which use just `inline`. `godot-cpp` uses `static inline` everywhere, and the `static inline` template function made my gdextension lib fail to build. Though I am not sure if it is a real error according to C++ standard or is just a clang bug with module, but I think `static inline` is not something appropriate in modern C++. So to be consistent I suggest that we put everything `constexpr` while possible. For the engine the only change is the two template functions.
author: zhehangd

commits.message: Mark template function in math_funcs_binary constexpr
